Hi x10an14,

Thank you for your pull request! I am sorry it took me a little while to take a proper look at it.

It looks like you made a typo and wrote `exist` instead of `exists`. I would prefer `path.exists` to `op.exists` but it is a matter of personal preference.

In the case of a list with some existent and some nonexistent filenames, the `load` still raises an error on the nonexistent files. I think it should just ignore the nonexistent filenames and error out if there are no existent filenames in the list. You already wrote the code, it is just commented out :)

I don't think a lack of valid filenames should be a TypeError since all the items in the list are the right type. I am leaning towards a ValueError or an IOError instead.

I think linesep might be useful elsewhere in the code. In which case, importing it in a larger scope would make sense.

Thanks again!

Gabbi provides a `poll` attribute in a test that makes it possible to do something like POST a job and then GET on the value of the returned location header until you get the expected response or time or number of allowed loops has run out. There's some information on that in the [poll section of this table](http://gabbi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html#response-expectations). Also there are some examples in gabbi's tests of itself, see, for example: https://github.com/cdent/gabbi/blob/master/gabbi/tests/gabbits_intercept/poll.yaml

In your specific example [jsonpath](http://gabbi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/jsonpath.html) could be used to inspect the returned json to get the id of the job and create the URL (using a [$RESPONSE substitution](http://gabbi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html#substitution)) to poll the job.

Isolation, setup and teardown in gabbi is per YAML file, not per test, because each YAML file is expected to be a sequence of connected calls that describe an interaction with an API:

* GET a /collection, see empty results
* POST to /collection, create a new item
* GET /collection/{id}, prove it is there
* PUT /collection/{id}, update it
* DELETE /collection/{id}, delete it
* GET /collection/{id}, prove it is not there

That sort of thing.

There are [fixtures](http://gabbi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/fixtures.html) that operate per YAML file which can be used to establish things like a persistence layer and any resources that need to exist.

If you need to test GET of something, in isolation, then you would want a YAML file with one test (that does the get) associated with a fixture that creates (and destroys) the expected object. When I'm doing my own testing, I don't usually do it this way, I'd instead do a sequence like I've described above.

If this isn't enough help to get you rolling, let me know, either here on in #gabbi on freenode IRC. If you've got some existing code we can look at together we can probably come up with a reasonable way to test things or I can at least point you at some more examples.

Most users on Windows don't even have a C compiler installed, so shipping the Lua *sources* won't help them. And Windows doesn't have a package management system, so security updates for Lua won't happen anyway. Thus, only complete binary packages for Lupa that include Lua would solve the problem on their side. But pretty much all other systems have some form of package management, which makes Windows the special case, once again.

And I do agree that the source package should be self-contained and *ship* Lua sources (although not necessarily *use* them), and I also think that it would be good to have any binary wheels on PyPI be self-contained and readily pip installable, especially in order to support Lupa as a dependency.

NB的项目是 littlecodersh 大虾创造的 [itchat](https://github.com/littlecodersh/ItChat) ，wxpy 只是尝试优化接口而做了一层封装哈哈。

二维码图片消失的问题之前也有其他朋友提出过，这是因为 itchat 会在**登陆成功后删除**原先创建的二维码。具体逻辑请见 这里 的 login() 函数，大约在第60~65行。

```python
    # 到这里已经登陆成功
    if hasattr(loginCallback, '__call__'):
        r = loginCallback()
    else:
        utils.clear_screen()
        if os.path.exists(picDir or config.DEFAULT_QR):
            os.remove(picDir or config.DEFAULT_QR)
```

不过好在 itchat 分别提供了获取二维码和登陆成功的回调参数: qrCallback / loginCallback，这两个参数在 wxpy 中为了统一代码风格，被改名为 qr_callback / login_callback (Robot对象初始化时使用)。

所以，通过传入以上两个回调，即可实现获取到二维码后的额外操作，以及修改登陆成功后是否删除二维码文件等表现。

```python
from google.appengine.ext import ndb

def setup_func():
    """set up test fixtures"""
    ndb.get_context().set_cache_policy(False)

@ndb.transactional
def transactional_func():
    pass

def test_1():
    ndb.Key('foo', 'bar').delete()

def test_2():
    transactional_func()

test_1.nosegae_datastore_v3 = True
test_1.setup = setup_func
test_2.nosegae_datastore_v3 = True
test_2.setup = setup_func
```

So currently if you cancel a task an exception is thrown inside it. Could you not do the same thing with a linked task?

e.g.

```
import curio

async def countdown(n):
    while n > 0:
        print('T-minus', n)
        await curio.sleep(1)
        n -= 1

async def sabotage(n):
    await curio.sleep(n/2)
    raise Exception("I died")

async def launch_manager():
    launchpad = await curio.spawn(countdown(10))
    spy = await curio.spawn(sabotage(10))
    spy.link(launchpad) # Spy reports failures upward
    await launchpad.join()
```

Then when `spy` raises an exception a `LinkedTaskFailure` or similar named exception can be thrown in the `launchpad`

But, there are cases where you'll want to close without any delay. 
You may not want to wait for async close() to be scheduled.
Hence you'll need another sync close(). :(

Personally I don't like idea of the two different close()'s.
But we are faced with consequences of async programming..

1. Events are just dumb objects. Mouse events specially are just namedtuples. I don't see how they can possibly fail to pickle.
2. Hooking/unhooking has no relation to event playback. They are two different Windows APIs.

   $ snmptranslate.py  RUCKUS-ZD-WLAN-MIB::1.3.6.1.4.1..25053.1.2.2.1.1.2.1.1.1.6.44.197.211.51.83.176
   pysnmp.smi.error.SmiError: Excessive instance identifier sub-OIDs left at MibTableRow((1, 3, 6, 1, 4, 1, 25053, 1, 2, 2, 1, 1, 2, 1, 1), None): (176,)

Sometimes that happens when agent and manager implement different versions of the same MIB. Could you check if other versions of RUCKUS-ZD-WLAN exist?

I believe this error is a result of a bug in distribute/setuptools prior to Distribute 0.6.24 (or maybe 0.6.26). I forget specifically what the issue is, but I'm pretty sure the fix is to upgrade Distribute to a late, stable version.

The underlying cause is not that keyring requires hgtools (it doesn't), but that it does require pytest-runner during setup, which in turn requires hgtools during setup, and that chain of setup_requires triggers errors in older versions of setuptools/distribute.

Unfortunately, the only thing keyring can do to fix the issue is to not have the setup requires dependency (which also means it can't have setuptools resolve its test dependencies when setup.py is invoked).

I apologize for the long time in addressing the bug report. Please let me know if you find that updating Distribute doesn't resolve the issue.

for wcwidth. I might make a note of it in the readme that it deviates from the standard, as the

to, but I'm afraid thats far out of scope for wcwidth, it would
require interaction with a terminal driver.

Hi, thank you for posting the question. A sid identifies an 'inference session', events posted or facts asserted to the same session (using the same sid) will be used to infer an action. Consider the ruleset:

```ruby
Durable.ruleset :test do
  when_all c.first = m.t == "purchase",
                 c.second = m.amount > first.amount,
                 c.third = m.amount > second.amount,
                 c.fourth = m.amount > (first.amount + second.amount + third.amount) / 3 do
    puts "-> " + first.amount.to_s 
    puts "-> " + second.amount.to_s
    puts "-> " + third.amount.to_s 
    puts "-> " + fourth.amount.to_s
  end
  when_start do
    post :test, {:id => 1, :sid => 1, :t => "purchase", :amount => 100}
    post :test, {:id => 2, :sid => 1, :t => "purchase", :amount => 200}
    post :test, {:id => 3, :sid => 1, :t => "purchase", :amount => 300}
    post :test, {:id => 4, :sid => 1, :t => "purchase", :amount => 250}
  end
end
```

Four messages are required to meet the criteria. One for each clause. The rules engine needs to remember the messages, which have met the criteria. A 'sid' identifies the state where the messages are stored.

@zappfinger Did you include “modes” as arguments to the `;@ args` annotation? I made modes special and they don’t need to be included there. You only need to modify the `opcode` definition to accomodate modes --- they will be included as typical arguments.

E.g. if you have your annotation like this: `;@ args kkk,k,k` and you have two mode variables, then your opcode annotation should be like this: `opcode SomeName, 0, iiiiiii` (seven i’s).

# download their images

```

If `TestFred.fake_fred_call` is `False`, it behaves the way it used to and makes an actual call to Fred; on the other hand, if `TestFred.fake_fred_call` is `True` (default value), the response from Fred is faked by `unittest.mock` so that tests behave consistently irrespectively of the popularity of the series and whether internet and a Fred API key are available or not. The test functions now run much faster. They are also bigger (because they include the Fred responses) and I split them in more methods.

 - With the tests runnable without a Fred API key, that opens the door to Travis (`.travis.yml`, `requirements.txt`) which is a very nice service (free for open-source project) which runs the test on every commit (master or any other branch/pull request), so you can easily check that:
    1. no one breaks compatibility with one or more version of Python (I've set it up for 2.7, 3.3, and 3.4), and 
    2. pull requests do not introduce any regression. See https://travis-ci.org/elmotec/fredapi as an example. There's even a shields.io badge for it that I added.

 - Lastly, I've listed the `test_suite` in `setup.py` so that one can run `python setup.py test` to run the tests.

I've read a little and I will create a separate branch for each feature going forward.

In general, I'd recommend to use the RPE metrics for such comparisons. There you don't have to care so much about the absolute frame of reference:
```bash
evo_rpe tum MH05_groundtruth.txt msckf_MH05.txt -a --pose_relation angle_deg -p
```

The two tools behave completely different with their default parameters!
There's a reason why the used parameters are explicitly shown in the title of `evo_rpe` & `evo_rpe` and why there are conflict checks in `evo_res`: it's easy to forget what you're actually measuring. Unfortunately, `evaluate_rpe.py`'s behavior is not so obvious.

Please also be aware of the disclaimer in the README of this package :wink:

> **What it's not:** a 1-to-1 re-implementation of a particular evaluation protocol tailored to a specific dataset. 

---

From `python evaluate_rpe.py --help`:

```
  --max_pairs MAX_PAIRS
                        maximum number of pose comparisons (default: 10000,
                        set to zero to disable downsampling)
  --fixed_delta         only consider pose pairs that have a distance of delta
                        delta_unit (e.g., for evaluating the drift per
                        second/meter/radian)
  --delta DELTA         delta for evaluation (default: 1.0)
  --delta_unit DELTA_UNIT
                        unit of delta (options: 's' for seconds, 'm' for
                        meters, 'rad' for radians, 'f' for frames; default:
                        's')
```

The default behavior of this script is ~~weird~~ "interesting" (non-fixed delta) - changing `delta` and `delta_unit` changes absolutely nothing. This might be a bug, but I don't know. Check the code if you really need to know what it does.

The delta parameters only have an effect if you use `--fixed_delta`. "Seconds" is not supported by evo, I also don't think it makes a lot of sense as a delta unit for an odometry error.

---

An example that gives more or less similar results:

```
evo_rpe tum fr2_desk_groundtruth.txt fr2_desk_ORB.txt
evo_rpe tum fr2_desk_groundtruth.txt fr2_desk_ORB.txt --pose_relation angle_deg


python evaluate_rpe.py fr2_desk_groundtruth.txt fr2_desk_ORB.txt --delta 1 --delta_unit f --fixed_delta --verbose
```

Remove the `()` in `csend()`.

This is not a problem with `keyboard`, but simply how Python itself works. `csent` means the function itself, while `csent()` means "invoke the code from the function named 'csent', and replace this term with the returned value (None)". To add a hotkey you want just the raw function, not the result of its invocation.

Hi @amrutauk, the last version includes a new parser for WebVTT so it could give back different validation errors compared to old versions. I checked the captions you provided and found:
- First vtt file contains the string --> as part of the text in line 471. Specs of the format do not allow this and that is why it does not validate. It looks like that there are two captions missing a blank line between them.
- The second file in line 13 does not have a cue timing associated to the text. I see the problem is that it has a blank line between the cue timings and the text. Removing that blank like it parses well.

> A library of vectors, matrices, complex numbers or quaternions for sure needs to implement such a function (the square of the norm)

Just to be clear, `quaternion.norm` returns the "Cayley norm" (as noted in its docstring) which is the version *without* the square-root, whereas `quaternion.abs` returns the version *with* the square-root.  The latter is consistent with the python/numpy treatment of complex numbers, whereas I don't think an analog of `quaternion.norm` is present natively in python or numpy.  Unfortunately, it is true that "norm" means different things to different people, and I can see why there might be some confusion.  But this is a choice that has been made; it's too late to unmake it.  The best I can do is to document it.

> the sqr function on real numbers also is equal to the square of the norm, so we obtain a homogeneous interpretation and nice polymorphism.  In some situations the same algorithm on different fields can be written in the exact same way if we interpret the sqr function like that.

But we *already have* a homogeneous interpretation and nice polymorphism that applies to *every mathematical structure with a product that I've ever seen*: the "square" of an element is the product of an element with itself.  In fact, now that I look, I see that there is a function called `numpy.square` that operates on complex numbers just as I've said.  If you're looking for a way to create more confusion, introducing a new square or sqr function with a different interpretation would be a good way to do it — especially since the functionality you're looking for already exists, just under a different name than you want.

> one could expect sqr(q) to be q*q if we also have a group operation (which is not the case, for example, for 3D vectors).

3D vectors absolutely *do* have a group operation: it's called the [geometric product](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geometric_algebra#The_Geometric_Product) and it's actually the basis for the modern understanding of how quaternions work.  But more relevant to this case, 3D vectors *as mapped into quaternions* have a group operation — which is all that matters here.

OAuth2 was created in order to indirectly expose an account to a third-party website without revealing your credentials to the third-party. The OAuth2 authorization endpoint is `/api/oauth2/authorize`. Using `/api/auth/login` violates OAuth2 because a) it does not use the OAuth2 API to generate a bearer token, and b) it reveals credentials to the third-party outside of a resource-owner credentials grant. [This activity is not supported by Discord, is strongly recommended against, and is bannable if the user has 2FA enabled.](https://github.com/hammerandchisel/discord-api-docs/issues/69) The recommended alternative is to use the `localStorage` token.

The fonts are indeed missing the required glyphs. Since 0.3.0, rinohtype uses Deja Vu Serif as a fallback font, but it doesn't include Korean and Arabic glyphs either.

There is only a small selection of fonts available for use with rinohtype for now. Search for ["rinoh-typeface" on PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi?%3Aaction=search&term=rinoh-typeface&submit=search). 

It is fairly easy to create a new font package. You can find the sources for the font packages available from PyPI in [my GitHub repositories](https://github.com/brechtm?tab=repositories&q=rinoh-typeface). The rules for font packages are not documented yet, but here are the basics:

- the entry point name (see `setup.py`) needs to be the same as the typeface name converted to lowercase (so that rinohtype can find the typeface by the name entered in a style sheet file)
- font packages need to be named `rinoh-typeface-<TYPEFACE_ID>`, where `<TYPEFACE_ID>` can be derived from the entry point name using the `entry_point_name_to_identifier` function in [rinoh/resource.py](/brechtm/rinohtype/blob/master/src/rinoh/resource.py) (to allow automatically downloading the font from PyPI and installing it)

In a future release, rinohtype will allow using fonts installed in the operating system as well.

Hello @1a1a11a 

- A replica represents how many times a given key will be placed on the ring for a given node. So this affects the distribution of the keys across the ring.

- A vnode represents how many times the ring will be split between the nodes. If you had only 4 nodes, the ring would be split in 4. vnodes are here to get a more dense distribution of nodes around the ring by virtually inflating their number. This allows to break the range of keys affected when a node is removed or added from/to the ring.

Hope this helps :)

I think I'd rather the environment-variable setting happened in `tests/__init__.py` so that you don't have to set them yourself if your local machine is not set to have the locale the tests expect.

However, I think that what you've found has flushed out something in Babel that makes me a bit sad. In particular, [this line](https://github.com/python-babel/babel/blob/903898f97d1010f5bf39b6b6d7e153fc6c2cc15b/babel/dates.py#L33) - specifically that it happens at import time. I suppose it isn't that horrible, as you wouldn't normally expect LC_TIME to change during a program's execution, perhaps. Though Bable prefers LC_TIME over LC_ALL, which appears to violate the suggestion made by https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Locale which says
> LC_ALL - Overrides individual LC_* settings: if LC_ALL is set, none of the below have any effect.

(I know that Ubuntu is not the authority on this, but I couldn't find anything that was.)

So the fix for this seems to be to move the existing setting of environment variable to just before
```python

To answer an early question: I think you can get negtive pitch if you use a reflecting transform. i.e. if you do a `FT_Set_Transform` with a matrix which has a negative determinant. I haven't tesed this, but e.g. if you set up example_1 to use matrix = (-1 0, 0, 1) instead.

Only two examples do `FT_Set_Transform` at the moment. So, example_1 and wordle would break if they ever get extended to use `FT_Set_Transform` that way.

Here is a 4th problem with your script - it takes about 5 hours to run on my hardware. It is crazy to post example that needs 5 hours to run to see if there is a problem at all ( `range(10000000)`).

Anyway, here is the correction for the 2nd leak - you are calling freetype wrong - you should be doing this instead - note the `byref` part and the `destroy=True` part:

```
blyph = glyph.to_bitmap(ft.FT_RENDER_MODE_NORMAL, byref(ft.Vector(0, 0)), destroy=True)
```

Without the `byref`, the 16-byte `ft.Vector(0, 0)` occupies both the 8-byte pointer and the destroy= argument, in effect hard-coding `destroy=False`. Hence adding `destroy=` afterwards had no effect.

It is a fault that `freetype-py`does not check argument types and sizes as they go through the python-C bridge. In that sense the other python wrapper is better, as the other wrapper would crash and abort if you try to pass wrong types/sizes instead of silently letting you use freetype wrongly. I also see without `byref`is more "natural", and in fact how some of the examples are written, so I have added code to auto-detect the wrong way of calling freetype and auto-correct it. I shall push that as an enhancement as some point.

I am still looking at the first leak - but it is another user problem (i.e. your script is poor). Both of the leaks are due to `destroy=False`. (in the 2nd case, because of the size mis-interpretation of FT_Vector). It is in the freetype documentation that when `destroy=False`, that's what destroy=False means - you, the user, should clean up (because you want to keep the original). The user's script is responsible for doing a manual destroy. i.e. you are supposed to do something like this:

```
# make a copy of glyph._FT_Glyph, as it will be changed by glyph.stroke()
original = glyph._FT_Glyph # not correct way of copying
glyph.stroke(stroker, destroy=False)
... 
# do more stuff with the original
...
ft.FT_Done_Glyph(original)
```

I am still trying to figure out the correct syntax of copying and keeping the original in python. In C, you just do `original = glyph._FT_Glyph`, but that does not work correctly in python - the copy is also changed on exit from stroke.

Back to the 4th criticism - there is no need to do `range(10000000)` . You could just do `range(1)`, or just remove the loop. One of the non-standard build options of freetype allows freetype itself to detect memory leaks of user-programs that uses freetype - freetype itself tells you what and where the leaks are, AFTER your program finishes. Hence your program MUST run within reasonable time, certainly not 5 hours,  to use that feature!

The first leaks is a few hundred bytes, the 2nd about 1.5k, but if both are on, about 9k per loop (the first destroy=False affects the 2nd). It is just poorly-written, in so many ways.

@kootenpv It looks like you're in good hands here, and, like the other commenters, I'm not sure I have a handle on what you're trying to do, but I did want to throw one more idea into the ring. You mentioned you'd already tokenized your input. PEGs are designed to encompass tokenization and parsing in a single pass. Fortunately, Parsimonious recently gained the experimental ability to parse pre-tokenized input. So [TokenGrammar](https://github.com/erikrose/parsimonious/blob/master/parsimonious/grammar.py#L156) and [Token](https://github.com/erikrose/parsimonious/blob/master/parsimonious/utils.py#L32) might avail you.

Worst case, though, yes: if you make your top-level rule an alternation containing all your other possible starting rules, you should be able to parse any of the things in your example. The caching will make performance not so bad, though you could probably do even better if you factored out any repeated strings of "tokens" into their own rules: then the parser wouldn't have to start from the very top each time it tried a new branch of the alternation. Of course, this would change your final tree shape as well.

```
from twisted.python import log
from raven import Client

client = Client(dsn=SENTRY_DSN)

def log_sentry(dictionary):
    if dictionary.get('isError') and 'failure' in dictionary:
        failure = dictionary['failure']
        exc_type = failure.type
        exc_value = failure.value
        traceback = failure.getTracebackObject()
        client.captureException(exc_info=(exc_type, exc_value, traceback))

log.addObserver(log_sentry)
```

Please give examples of what you propose. No need to flesh out over the entire Kraken API method space. :)

-----

Also, turning Kraken API methods into package API methods means we could have some long-absent methods that _are_ present in Kraken's internal codebase, but _not_ as external API methods - such as the "available balance" method that's [currently available as an example](https://github.com/veox/python3-krakenex/blob/b66a413958a90057deb5d0aa2e0cb9ba2c24d95f/examples/print-available-balances.py) (note the `FIXME`).

-----

`krakenex` has been at `MAJOR` version zero since forever - mainly because it hasn't seen much use (well, _I_ haven't seen it used much). E.g., check the [issue opening dates](https://github.com/veox/python3-krakenex/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue) - most of them have been opened this year.

> Has there been a decision to not include individual python methods for each API method?

Although Kraken's API method names haven't changed since inception (AFAIR), I've decided against it (so far); since, if they _did_ change, that would be tech debt put on me.

However, that was my thoughts up to this year, when many more people seem to have started using it. This pushed me to work toward a `v1.0.0` release, that should formalise the package's own API change process.

> If this is not the case, are there any plans to implement corresponding python methods anytime soon?

I do not plan to do it myself any time soon - not a priority for me.

Getting `v1.0.0` out the door is primary. Getting `requests` in `v2.0.0` follows. Changing API structure can follow after that.

Also, I'd like to retain `query_{public,private}()` as low-level functionality - since, after all, that's what Kraken's API has as low-level "endpoints".

Look for the line just before the progress bar. It'll show the IP address and port of the other side of the connection, an arrow to indicate whether this client made an outbound connection (`->`) or accepted an inbound one (`<-`), and `relay:` if it's using the relay:

```
Receiving (->relay:tcp:transit.magic-wormhole.io:4001)..
```

The encryption will be CPU bound, but unless you're using a small machine like a Raspberry Pi, I wouldn't expect that to be the limitation.

Note, you can force the clients to use the relay (for comparison) by using `--no-listen` on both sides, like `wormhole send --no-listen FILENAME` and `wormhole rx --no-listen`. That disables the listening socket, leaving the relay as the only remaining way to make a transfer connection.

And, depending upon your network topology, be aware that what wormhole does is to enumerate all the local IP addresses (basically it runs `ifconfig` and parses the output), and send them all (encrypted) to the other side. That could fail to establish a direct connection for any of the following reasons:

* `ifconfig` isn't installed
* it can't parse the `ifconfig` output properly (maybe yours has some new format)
* the local-interface addresses aren't actually reachable from the other peer (your network might use VLANs to isolate each port of that switch, or one of them might have a statically-assigned IP that the other one can't reach for some reason)

The connection manager just tries all the addresses it knows about and uses whichever connection is established first. I would expect that to be the fastest one, but it doesn't actually measure the speed or anything. So I could imagine a weird network in which some hard-coded port-forwarded addresses looked viable but actually required hairpin-NAT or something beyond the local switch, and for whatever reason it manages to pick that one instead of the "local" RFC1918 address.

Anyways, hopefully that'll give you enough information to investigate the problem. I know we should probably improve the `ifconfig` routine (since on linux the `ip` tool is now the preferred one, so at least we should try running that in addition to `ifconfig`). Another potential thing to improve is libsodium's `SecretBox` encryption speed, if that ends up being a limiting factor here. It's also worth experimenting with the segment size: by default we write 16KiB to the socket each time.. maybe the network is so fast (compared to the CPU) that we should raise that and see if it helps anything (I wouldn't expect it to, but you never know). Look at CHUNK_SIZE in `twisted/protocols/basic.py` in the `FileSender` class, and add something like `fs.CHUNK_SIZE = 2**20` after the `fs = basic.FileSender()` in `src/wormhole/cli/cmd_send.py` around line 375.

Ah, interesting. Let's see, allowing the same code to be reused multiple times also allows an attacker multiple guesses. We could mitigate this by making multiple-use codes longer: if you allow two uses, add one bit of entropy, if you allow 16 uses, add four bits, etc. Three words would be enough to cover 256 uses without improving the attacker's overall probability of success.

You couldn't allow an unbounded number of recipients, even with a timeout, since an attacker could just exhaustively test the entire namespace until they find something that works. So we'd need a finite number of recipients in addition to the time limit. (And the time limit is a good UX thing, since with the single recipient you know when you're done, whereas with multiple recipients you probably won't).

The trickier part is that the "nameplate" (the numeric channel identifier at the beginning of the wormhole code: the "4" in "4-purple-sausages") is used to identify which "mailbox" or queue the client is using. If three or more clients all use the same nameplate, their messages will get jumbled up.

So we'd need some change to the protocol to allow this to work. Maybe the sender doesn't put anything in the mailbox at first, and waits to see a PAKE message from one of the receivers. Then the sender would need to narrowcast a PAKE message for just that one receiver (while continuing to listen for PAKE from other receivers). It'd be nice if these could use separate mailboxes.. I think the server would need to be involved.

Let me think about it.. maybe it's possible.

OK so I feel like I should set a few things straight.

First of all, I’m 0 on attrs inclusion in any form since it would probably mean more headaches for me – **but** I completely agree that to encourage proper OO design, writing proper classes should be more straightforward in Python.  That’s why I’m here trying to help.  To help, not push my ego or agenda or whatever – I really don’t feel like I have a dog in this fight. 

***

One thing I’d like to stress is that many more people gave their input into `attrs`’s design evolution than on this PEP.  So if you want to re-build my shed with colors that are more pleasing to your taste: I have no problem with that and will not fight you at all.  The first rule of `attrs` is that it pops out regular classes.  Thus there’s no lock-in and there’s no downside for me if you go your own way. 

However I would strongly encourage you to stick to `attrs`’s **design and priciples** instead of reinventing the wheel from bottom up unless you come up with *good* reasons to diverge of course.  Most of it’s design & features did not emerge from a vacuum but evolved from long discussions and there’s no need to replicate those here with fewer people, no matter how much smarter they are.  As you may have noticed, your discussion tend to converge to the same results anyway.  Also feel free to ask about our design decisions, chances are there’s a good reason for it.  We’ve put a lot of effort into not painting ourselves into a corner, so be careful to not paint there yourself just for the sake of doing things differently.

***
Another thing that somehow keeps repeated and annoys me is PEP 526.  As I wrote before, [this already works in a PoC](https://github.com/python-attrs/attrs/issues/214):

```python
>>> import attr

>>> @attr.s
... class C:

>>> C(1)
C(x=1, y=42)

I looked into this more.

My suspicion about "revocation endpoint will now return empty string in response body" was wrong, that happens after where the exception / backtrace starts.

The change in oauthlib that causes this change in behavior is this:

oauthlib 0.7.2:
```
    def validate_revocation_request(self, request):
        """..."""
        if not request.token:
            raise InvalidRequestError(request=request,
                                      description='Missing token parameter.')

        if not self.request_validator.authenticate_client(request):
            raise InvalidClientError(request=request)
...
```

oauthlib 1.0.3:
```
    def validate_revocation_request(self, request):
        """..."""
        if not request.token:
            raise InvalidRequestError(request=request,
                                      description='Missing token parameter.')

        if self.request_validator.client_authentication_required(request):
            if not self.request_validator.authenticate_client(request):
                raise InvalidClientError(request=request)
...
```

So it's actually the fact that `client_authentication_required()` in Flask-Oauthlib is now being called, and that always returns False if grant_type is not included in the request, so then `authenticate_client()` is not called, so it doesn't set `request.client`.

I'm not sure if this is a good idea, but it looks like another possible fix is changing the line that throws the exception to `if tok and tok.client_id == request.client_id:` because it looks like (for my cases) client_id does exist in the request object (because it's in the POST body, for my application anyway).

Looking at your use-case (custom create_revision context managers), it looks like you can do this already without changing django-reversion at all!

``` pyy
from contextlib import contextmanager
from reversion import create_revision, set_user, add_meta

@contextmanager
def create_revision_for_your_obj(user, status):
    with create_revision():
        set_user(user)
        add_meta(YourObjMeta, status=status)
        yield

with create_revision_for_your_obj(request.user, 'draft'):
        obj.save()

``

My suggestion would be to make strict restore available as an optional feature now with the Javascript client-side hack to produce reasonable behaviour by default – quick and dirty as it is – then use that as an example for the proposed new default behaviour once the server-side can be sorted out.

* adding custom Javascript to the admin page to disable form fields on the client-side

> As a general guideline, strict mode (roughly) accepts only keys that you can generate with recent openssh's ssh-keygen, loose mode should accept keys openssh accepts.

This link explains the math behind the key size: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/12192116/rsa-bitlength-of-p-and-q

> For something more realistic, like a 1024-bit modulus, then yes, you're pretty safe choosing two 512-bit primes p and q at random: that is, choose p and q uniformly from the set of all primes in the range [2^511, 2^512].

> Of course, if you do choose two 512-bit primes, you'll end up with either a 1023-bit or a 1024-bit modulus; that's probably not worth worrying about, but if you really cared about getting exactly a 1024-bit modulus you could either restrict the range of p and q further, say to [1.5 * 2^511, 2^512], or just throw out any 1023-bit modulus and try again.

OpenSSH implementation (and many others I know) forces the creation of 1024 (default) / 768 (minimum) bit keys by choosing p and q in the proper interval (the final key size is what is important). Putty seems to focus in the p and q size (the prime sizes is what is important) and can generate 1023 / 767 keys in some circumstances.

To be complient with OpenSSH implementation (and others like Mikrotik/Cisco), the original values (1024/768) should be kept.

@masoudr I have also encountered exactly the same problem as @cmlyldz . And I used  `VS2015 x86 Native Tools Command Prompt` to compile `dlib`, but the problem didn't go.

If you compile `dlib` manually with CUDA support (after having installed CUDA and cuDNN), it will use the GPU for generating the face embeddings. So that's a bit faster. However the part that still isn't GPU-accelerated is finding the locations of the faces in each image.

Also the face_recognition script isn't caching anything, so it's rescanning all the known_images each time you run the program which is slower than it needs to be.

One way to speed things up a little bit would be to change the script to pre-cache the face embeddings for each known image and save them somewhere:

1. Cache the results of running `face_recognition.face_encodings(unknown_image)[0]` on each known image. You only need to do that once.
2. When you want to test an unknown picture, load those cached results into an array called `cached_known_faces` or something and pass that to `face_recognition.compare_faces([cached_known_faces], unknown_face_encoding)`.

You are right, it is U+03B1 (GREEK SMALL LETTER ALPHA). It is an example of a character not in my charset, but which I can write on my keyboard. I naively used it as a general example of such character not considering Greek charset since I was viewing it as “mathematical alpha” since that is my usage.

The general point is that a codepage encodes only 256 characters, but you may enter 65536 characters to cmd.exe, which fails silently – e.g. greek β is converted to german ß, while α is turned into ?. And this compat conversion is done before it gets to sys.stdin decoder, so it is not caused directly by sys.stdin.encoding. So unless you start with Greek locale, just chaging sys.stdin.encoding won't help.

Recursion was indeed not handled well. Fixed implementation and added a attribute lookup that will not be retrieved from logging module. (Sorry for polluting the history, should have run tox locally. Python 2.6 coding has been ages ago)

The combination of ``.isalpha()`` and ``.isupper()`` basically allows access to all log levels, but not to other attributes that don't fall into that pattern, like ``._STYLES`` or ``.BASIC_FORMAT``.

Custom loglevels, added for example via ``logging.setLevelName(13, 'custom')``, are stored in ``logging._levelNames`` (python2) or ``logging._nameToLevel`` and ``logging._levelToName`` (python3).

The logging module itself uses ``.getLevelName()`` as [described here](https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html#logging.getLevelName) to get a textual representation for a level. Illustrated by doing ``logging.getLevelName(123456)`` which returns ``Level 123456``. 

I would not go down the rabbit hole of looking up custom log levels. It's [discouraged by python](https://docs.python.org/2/howto/logging.html#custom-levels) and adds complexity. If people insist on custom log levels they can always import ``logging`` itself in the tests.

Looking back at previous commits I actually think the explicit list of levels was better (because: explicit) than the ``isalpha()``, ``isupper()`` combo (or ``lvl == lvl.upper()`` for that matter). Those levels haven't changed ever and are based on good practices.

Did you notice the "client is gone" message a few lines above the error? That means that the server did not receive a PING packet in more than the "ping timeout" period which is one minute.

> What if a client quietly dies and we will never send a message to this socket again

If the client is connected over websocket, there is not "quiet" dying. The disconnection is detected immediately and the disconnect handler should be invoked.

For a client that is connected over long-polling, things are more complicated. The only way the client can die is by just suddenly not sending GET and POST requests anymore. Now, because of the long-polling, when the client effectively goes away, the system should be currently processing the last polling GET request this client sent. The response for this request is going to timeout, because the client isn't sending the POST requests that have the heartbeat. The socket will be closed when the timeout happens, in 60 seconds by default.

Maybe there is a race condition, in this situation, I'll have to review the long-polling path carefully.

Please create a **new** issue.  But I will go ahead and comment here.

`$$ ... $$` is already possible. There is no restriction on spaces between `$$`.

I can consider the advanced mode for inline, but it will need ArithMatex's  non-generic mode. Discussed later in this comment.

## No Issue with `\}`

There is no issue with \}. Let me explain why.

The examples posted here worked with one understandable issue.  The 1st and 3rd were not parsed as expected.  This is because \\ is treated as a Markdown escape, but then placed back in the HTML as a single `\`.  Then MathJax parses it because it is looking for `\(...\)`.

```
\\( \forall i, \, {\mathcal{F} \left( g \right)}_{i} \in \left\{ -1, 1 \right\} \\)

\( \forall i, \, {\mathcal{F} \left( g \right)}_{i} \in \left\{ -1, 1 \right\} \)

\\(\forall i, \, {\mathcal{F} \left( g \right)}_{i} \in \left\{ -1, 1 \right\}\\)

\(\forall i, \, {\mathcal{F} \left( g \right)}_{i} \in \left\{ -1, 1 \right\}\)
```

What you actually need to do is double escape because Markdown will take `\\\\(` and turn it into HTML like this `\\(` and then MathJax will ignore it. Makes sense?  Even curly brackets that are escaped are expected to translate from `\{` to `{` if not handled specially.  So this does not surprise me. Take a look at how other Markdown parsers handle this: https://johnmacfarlane.net/babelmark2/?text=%5C%7Btest%5C%7D+%5C%5C.

So when escaping by properly with `\\\\(...\\\\)`.  Let's look at the results:

![screenshot 2018-05-26 12 55 47](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1055125/40579450-9e678c98-60e4-11e8-802a-0c9552ecf61a.png)

Now Markdown Preview has another extension enabled called `pymdownx.escapeall` which will allow  any character to be escaped. So you'll probably see additional things get coverted like `\f` --> `f`.  But you can disable that if you find it surprising:

![screenshot 2018-05-26 13 00 39](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1055125/40579466-d2a2570e-60e4-11e8-9a91-f35c4f6bac44.png)

## Non Generic Mode

But here's a trick. You can use Arithmatex's non generic mode (when the `generic` option is set to `False` which is the default) to actually wrap stuff in special MathJax scripts, which MathJax looks for by default.  Then you don't even need to setup your JavaScript config to look for silly things like `\(...\)` in your HTML.  Then escaping like `\\(...\\)` will work fine.  As mentioned earlier, this would be required when using `\$`.  Arithmatex does not process `\$` even in generic mode. It treats it special, so `\$` becomes `$` in HTML MathJax will process it anyways unless you let it look for the scripts instead.

You can actually configure your own config in Markdown Preview and point to it instead.  This one doesn't look for plain text to convert, but looks for the special non-generic MathJax scripts.

```js
MathJax.Hub.Config({
  config: ["MMLorHTML.js"],
  jax: ["input/TeX", "output/HTML-CSS", "output/NativeMML"],
  extensions: ["MathMenu.js", "MathZoom.js"],
  TeX: {
    extensions: ["AMSmath.js", "AMSsymbols.js"],
    TagSide: "right",
    TagIndent: ".8em",
    MultLineWidth: "85%",
    equationNumbers: {
      autoNumber: "AMS",
    },
    unicode: {
      fonts: "STIXGeneral,'Arial Unicode MS'"
    }
  },
  displayAlign: "left",
  showProcessingMessages: false,
  messageStyle: 'none'
});
```
I actually tried to drop generic mode originally, but users requested it back again to work with non-MathJax libs.  It probably makes sense for Markdown Preview to not use generic mode by default.

Why would we need a separate class for mermaid?

If mermaid is an alternative flowchart/sequence diagram renderer, you don't **need** a special class, you would just point mermaid at the existing blocks instead using the suggested JavaScript libraries I note in the documentation.  SuperFences doesn't care what the actual content under flowchart or sequence-diagram is. For mermaid, this manual feeding of the content might not be possible, so for a more simple approach, I could probably optionally output the content under a div and allow this to be more accessible by other libraries.

I don't imagine I will be adding a `mermaid` specific classes, but maybe I could allow outputting content under a div.  In the case of mermaid, you would just need to point it at the flowchart class and/or sequence diagram class.

I would appreciate if we could  have some kind of caching between the different filtered lists (active, valid, ..). Parsing my leases files already takes a lot of time and doing it twice isn't ideal.

Regarding the deprecation of `get()`:
The class has previously only been a container for the file that we are parsing and not much more. 
If you do not want to break current users code then we should maybe move to a different kind of architecture?

A simple methods retrieving all the leases would probably be sufficient. We could return a Result-DHCPLeases-Type which just holds all the parsed leases. The class could be sub-classed based on LeaseV4 or LeaseV6. On the Result-Type we could offer various "filters" like the currently provided "current". Also filters like "active" and maybe "inactive" would be of use.

I could imagine the user code to look like this:

```python

leases = dhcp_leases.parse('/var/lib/dhcpd/leases.db')

type(leases) in [LeasesV4, LeasesV6]

# access properties providing lazily calculated lists of leases
leases.current
leases.active
leases.inactive

# some filters (just quick ideas so far)
leases.from_hardware('aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff')
leases.for('192.2.0.145')

# we could even make them chainable without much effort
l = leases.for('192.2.0.145')
l.valid
l.invalid
l.current
```

Hmm, ok. Here are some high-level thoughts...

### Functional requirements

1. The _primary use-case_ for this library is basically to as simply as possible dump some data from Pandas into an interactive control to quickly explore. 
2. The _reverse-path use-case_ (i.e. after UI interaction, get the reformated data back out) is less important to me, and I think it could be accomodated via a special Renderer (i.e. instead of a table, you get a textarea with a copy-pasteable pandas call to regenerate the same table as a dataframe?). 
3. The _sharing use-case_ (i.e. defining the pivot table parameters in Python and exporting to HTML) I value more than the reverse-path use-case. 

### Non-function requirements

1. ease of installation
2. writing to disk and/or relying on a CDN is brittle

### Design tradeoffs

|  | current | widget | Bokeh embedding |
|---------|-------|-----|------|
| primary use-case | yes | yes | yes |
| sharing use-case | no | no | yes |
| reverse-path use-case | no | yes | no |
| ease of installation | light | heavy | light |
| disk/cdn | bad | ok | ok |

Based on this, I think I'm leaning more towards exploring a Bokeh-style embedding approach than a widget...

It looks like it is not a bug, but a configuration error regarding the numerical libraries. spaCy makes use of CV2, which causes this problem.

The Github of Tensorflow talked about this problem: https://github.com/tensorflow/models/issues/523
And it cited this thread in StackOverflow: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/19268293/matlab-error-cannot-open-with-static-tls

The way to do it is to reload the library, or reinstall Tensorflow etc. To work around, load opencv2, or spaCy first before loading the library.

When the DAG is parsed and the scheduler figures out that a task instance needs to run, it's placed into the `SCHEDULED` state. Only tasks in the `SCHEDULED` state get sent to the executor. Once the task gets sent to the executor, it's placed into the `QUEUED` state. Since only tasks in the `SCHEDULED` state get sent to the executor, it eliminates the problem of the same task getting sent to the executor multiple times.

> I have a question about Fido's contract.
>
>    - We are adding a gzip_enabled python parameter but we expect the
>    caller to gzip the body. The we expect Fido to set the HTTP
>    'Content-Encoding' header and take care of decompressing the response and
>    reading the header. So we have the caller responsible for compressing and

>    decompressing the response while adding and removing the header

We sure have a big mess by now... ;-)

Thinking of it now, having a external library that might have a direct influence on the data base layout of your models, is it really a good idea? I don't know. 

But it sure is not be a lot of code in this case:
```python
from django.db import models
from django.utils.timezone import now

class TimeStampedModel(models.Model):
    created = models.DateTimeField(_('created'), default=now,
                                   editable=False, blank=True)
    modified = models.DateTimeField(_('modified'), default=now,
                                    editable=False, blank=True)

    def save(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.modified = now()
        super(TimeStampedModel, self).save(*args, **kwargs)

    class Meta:
        abstract = True
```

With this bit of code, I can remove django-extensions from the production dependencies altogether, and just keep it as as  developer tool. This is also where this library really rocks: `runserver_plus` and `shell_plus`. And as a dev tool, it doesn't always need to be 100% _production ready_.
This is an issue with the readme of a third-party library, so I think this fix should go upstream.

Looks good. Some high level feedback:

1. With these types of abstractions, it's often nice to be able to get the requests that were sent through the stubber class. Do you think it would be useful to have some way to be able to ask the stubber what requests it stubbed, and then in turn some way to clear the history? In the PHP SDK, we just track the last request and last response -- https://github.com/aws/aws-sdk-php/blob/master/src/MockHandler.php
2. What do you think about adding support for stubbing errors so that when invoked, the class raises a pre-created exception?
3. Do you think there would be any value in allowing different strategies for determining which response to use when stubbing? For example, one could just pop from a queue (what you've implemented), while another strategy might be to just call a function where a user could do various things in a test each time a call is stubbed. Another use case might be a strategy that has some predetermined expectation on what requests will be sent and the response associated with them. We didn't include this type of functionality in the PHP SDK because it is really easy to create anonymous functions that act as the HTTP client.
4. Instead of my third bullet-point (and possibly in addition to...), what about adding functions to the queue as well? When a function is popped of the queue the function is invoked with the request params and the return value is the result of the function (or an exception is raised)? Using functions might be required for raising exceptions if the exception cannot be created in advance (because it needs a reference the request or something).

I'm not familiar with pygit2, and certainly not familiar with Python's FFI, but it looks like this API is designed to not ask for file patches to be generated, just to perform a tree diff.  File patches should be lazily generated when you request them.  Unless I've misunderstood what I'm read, I think that there's a couple of possibilities:

1. pygit2 is inadvertently invoking file patch generation on the tree diff before handing it back to you.
2. Your code is inadvertently invoking some method that requests file patch generation.
3. Neither of these two things is happening, it's just slow for another reason.  (libgit2 brain damage, incredibly slow marshaling of strings, etc, etc.)

I suspect it's _not_ 2 and that your code is obvious, but would you mind sharing just so that we have a baseline to start from?  It would be nice if one of the pygit2 maintainers could jump in and help with clarifying point 1.  If that's no help then we may need to explore other options.

The `Element Text Should Not Be Empty` is not general enough to qualify as a keyword. But having a counterpart, but a negative one for `Element Text Should Be` might be possible. But have you considered that there are possibility to check the element text in other ways too. Example:
```
| ${text] = | Get Text | xpath://path/to/element | 
| Should Not Contain | ${text} | None |
```
Please remember that Selenium will return [Python None](https://docs.python.org/2/library/constants.html#None) when element does not have text and also when element is not visible (even the element has text).  Therefore the number of false positive may be quite high. And in all UI tests, proving that something is not there, is actually quite complicated problem.  

But I am not against writing a new keyword `Element Text Should Not Be`, would you be willing to provide PR for such keyword?

Starting from 3.0.0 Alpha release the architecture of the [SeleniumLibrary](https://github.com/robotframework/SeleniumLibrary/blob/master/README.rst) has changed. The [Selenium2Library](https://github.com/robotframework/Selenium2Library) was renamed as SeleniumLibrary and currently Selenium2Library is only wrapper on top of the SeleniumLibrary. Many things has changed and it is possible that libraries extending SeleniumLibrary needs some changes.

Now there are many ways to solve the problem, but there is not a single solution that would fit for all. Could you share a simple example that demonstrates the problem? With some example code and test data that demonstrates the problem? And seeing the error message would be a goof start. 

Please note that old releases are not deleted from the PyPI and the [1.8.0](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/robotframework-selenium2library/1.8.0) is the last release using the old architecture. Doing `pip install robotframework-selenium2library==1.8.0` is install you the correct version.

I like the idea that you're keeping the _vendor code separate - that's a good plan.

Since cmp_version is a published package, it's probably best if we declare it as a vendored dependency - that is, in `pkg_resources/_vendor/vendored.txt`, declare the dependency for cmp_version, update pavement.py, and then run `paver update_vendored` (which is how all of the vendored dependencies are incorporated). Finally, instead of importing from `pkg_resources._vendor`, import from `pkg_resources.extern`. Doing this enables other package managers to treat cmp_version as a proper dependency and omit it from the vendored installation.

But more importantly - pkg_resources already relies on packaging, and I'd be really surprised if `packaging` doesn't already provide a suitable sort-order for these package version numbers.

```
>>> from pkg_resources.extern import packaging
>>> packaging.version
<module 'pkg_resources.extern.packaging.version' from '/Users/jaraco/Dropbox/code/main/setuptools/pkg_resources/_vendor/packaging/version.py'>
>>> low_ver = packaging.version.parse('2.7.2')
>>> high_ver = packaging.version.parse('2.7.10')
>>> low_ver < high_ver
True
```

This appears to be a missing system directory in the virtualenv:

* `/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages` is used in the virtualenv.
* `/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/site-python` is **not** used.

The second path is a standard location for system packages, as reported by Python itself:

So, I was hoping I wouldn't need to jump into the bikeshed wagon, but here I go... :D

The problem of using `None` is that it will give rise to this kind of code:

    if sel.css('a').attrib:
        something = sel.css('a').attrib['something']

with all its variants:

    something = sel.css('a').attrib['something] if sel.css('a').attrib else None
    something = (sel.css('a').attrib or {}).get('something')

I would also bet that the null-check would be done after hitting the problem (possibly in production).

It may not seem like a big deal, but forcing all users to add a null-check to guarantee their code won't break just because of an use-case that is likely not that common, it makes for a counter-intuitive API. Moreover, users that actually need to distinguish between empty selectors will probably check `sel.css('a')` already anyway.

I hereby invoke the motto `practicality beats purity`. :)

The main reason we're adding `attrib` to `SelectorList` is for it to be a shortcut, which means we're already thinking "practicality beats purity" -- we wouldn't want to return attributes for a list if we were caring more about the type consistency than the usability.

TL;DR: I believe that 1) always returning a dict will make it easier for users to write robust code for the most common case, and that 2) returning `None` will not make the resulting API any cleaner (nor dirtier, either) and is more prone to errors.

Does this make sense?

Hi @zhanghan987. The producer includes [retry logic](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/bddcc4206a2688dccfd4df25716d4380520e883c/pykafka/producer.py#L466) that is used by the worker threads that send messages to brokers. You're correct that the entire message batch (in your example, 100 messages) would be lost when an unhandled error such as yours is encountered in the producer's worker thread. To handle this, I recommend keeping recently produced messages in memory and checking them against `Producer.get_delivery_report()`. If you fail to receive a delivery report for a given message after a crash, you can have your program retry that message.

@kbourgoin and I talked about this when I added the single buffer. The whole idea with the single buffer is do only ever do a single memory allocation, so allowing it to automatically resize kind of defeats that purpose. As of now, I'm in favor of forcing users to set the buffer size at the start. That's how the JVM consumer works, and I'd like to maintain as close parity with it as possible.

That said, it does sound possible that there's a bug here - 64MB is a big number.
(PS: I guess all the various ways you listed that make the hangs go away all have in common that they slow down the rate of fetching - so that's sort of consistent with this?)

One caveat to this fix, which is mentioned in the commit message: the consumer code @eltermann provided will need to change slightly. Since this commit makes it possible for `consume()` to return `None` during a rebalance even if `consumer_timeout_ms=-1` and `block=True`, you'll need to make sure that your consumer code can handle the result of `consume()` being `None`.     message = consumer.consume()
    if message is not None:  # this is new
        print(message.value)
```

Hi @Landau1908!

It is indeed possible! In fact if you `plt.plot(ecg_skimage)`, you'll see that you don't have all zeros at all, just very tiny numbers.

You've been bitten, as so many have, by scikit-image's implicit type conversions:

http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/user_guide/data_types.html

We convert your input data, which is int32, to float, by dividing by the maximum int32, which is `2**31 - 1`. That's why your resulting array has such tiny values.

Here's what I get when I try:

```python
In [13]: plt.plot(ecg)
Out[13]: [<matplotlib.lines.Line2D at 0x1324afb00>]

In [14]: plt.plot(ecg_skimage * (2**31 - 1))
Out[14]: [<matplotlib.lines.Line2D at 0x12b5848d0>]
```
![figure_1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/492549/36769723-4181b74e-1c9a-11e8-8886-4aab645638e4.png)

Not sure that's very good denoising, but you can tweak the parameters now instead of trying to figure out what went wrong. =)

We definitely need to figure out how to make our data type conversions friendlier. If you have any ideas, please add them to this issue, or raise a new one:

https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/1234

In the meantime I'll close this one. Thanks! (And thank you very much for the sample code, I would have been unable to debug your problem without it!)

    + Satellite ID (1 byte) (actual value from ICD, not value minus 1)

It's a bit hard to follow what exactly is the problem. Could you possibly try to amend with an example? I understand you want to update the URLs, but I don't understand why? 

My unfiltered thoughts:
- *Don't change history:* Past records revisions (i.e. ``record.revisions``) should not be editable in anyway. Editing the revision history would be like rewriting your entire git commit log, because an URL changed. Tampering with the history of a record would be bad record management IMHO.
- *Published JSON schemas should have very persistent URLs* which are independent of organisation renaming etc (take DataCite or Dublin Core XML schemas as examples). This could e.g. be achieved by referencing schemas using persistent identifiers such as PURLs, DOIs or EPICs.
- *Historic vs active data:* Record revisions is in my opinion history data. When you talk about changing URLs in past revisions, it makes me think that you consider the revisions as "active data".

Ah, yes. Py3 does support a timeout there, but Py2 doesn't. The subprocess module in Py2 is pretty poor overall, but much better in Py3. But you should really never call wait unless you want to block. That's why it's called "wait". Use poll method if you don't want to block. If the main thread is blocking forever then something isn't right with the process management... You might have to read from it first to drain the pipe that's it's blocked on. You're also not getting the exit status, so the main thread won't know if the subprocess terminated normally or not. @mih - I think the problem is simply that you are using the wrong URI for the schema.org context. Per https://schema.org/docs/howwework.html it should be https://schema.org/docs/jsonldcontext.json . With this in place your example works:

```
>>> from rdflib import Graph
INFO:rdflib:RDFLib Version: 4.2.1
>>> test_json = """
... {
...   "@context": "https://schema.org/docs/jsonldcontext.json",
...   "@type": "Person",
...   "name": "Jane Doe",
...   "jobTitle": "Professor",
...   "telephone": "(425) 123-4567",
...   "url": "http://www.janedoe.com"
... }"""
>>> g = Graph().parse(data=test_json, format='json-ld')
>>> print(g.serialize(format="nt").decode('utf-8'))
_:Nbe6149bb453a44afb7e83a8ae4328026 <http://schema.org/jobTitle> "Professor" .
_:Nbe6149bb453a44afb7e83a8ae4328026 <http://schema.org/telephone> "(425) 123-4567" .
_:Nbe6149bb453a44afb7e83a8ae4328026 <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type> <http://schema.org/Person> .
_:Nbe6149bb453a44afb7e83a8ae4328026 <http://schema.org/name> "Jane Doe" .
_:Nbe6149bb453a44afb7e83a8ae4328026 <http://schema.org/url> <http://www.janedoe.com/> .
```

The result from a rally.get(...) operation is a RallyRESTResponse instance.  That instance has an iterator characteristic so you can do either of:
first_item = rally.get('Story').next()
or
response = rally.get('Story')
all_stories = [item for item in response]
story_one = all_stories[0]
story_last = all_stories[-1]

I have no current plans to support indexing, but I'd be interested in learning about how you'd use it if it existed.  If the iterator protocol  doesn't meet your needs, I'm open to a clear explanation of how indexing is the right solution for your problem domain.

Hello!  Yes this is an issue because `psutil` uses C libraries. 

There are some ways around this but are somewhat painful depending on how you are deploying your lambda.

If you make use of Serverless, there is a plugin for it named `serverless-python-requirements` that has a `dockerizePip` flag that will build your lambda inside of an Amazon Linux docker container, which solves the issue.

So the TL;DR is to build your Lambda inside of an Amazon Linux docker, and find a way to grab out the  built ZIP -- OR -- send us a PR to remove psutil with something equivalent that doesn't require the C libraries.

I thought I documented this but I guess not. The easiest (though also slowest) way is to install [circleci local](https://circleci.com/docs/2.0/local-jobs/) and run `circleci build`. Unfortunately this is not 100% the same as online, but it's close enough.

To run for a specific browser using your local (non-dockerized) environment, set these two env variables:

* `ARSENIC_SERVICE`
* `ARSENIC_BROWSER`

Both take the class name of either a service or a browser as a value, with optionally query-string/json encoded arguments to pass to that class.

For example to run against chrome (using chromedriver) in headless mode, set them to:

* `ARSENIC_SERVICE=Chromedriver`
* `ARSENIC_BROWSER=Chrome?chromeOptions=%7B%22args%22%3A+%5B%22--headless%22%2C+%22--disable-gpu%22%2C+%22--no-sandbox%22%5D%7D`

Then run `pytest` in the root directory.

If you have the required tools installed, I recommend the non-docker version because of speed and debugability.

@gamaanderson After reflecting on this topic this evening I'm fully in support of adding better support in Py-ART for encoding variable as integers when writing to NetCDF files.  As you pointed out this is a standard procedure in the field and is transparently supported in the NetCDF4 Python library.  In addition, downstream tools may be expected integer encoded moments which need to be supported.  Even is truncation and compression results in smaller files at time, this method is not common nor as widely supported.  

I still think the Radar object should store data as floats after performing any scaling and offset calculation.  The Radar class sits at the top of the abstraction of numerous radar data sources and is expected to independent of the original source of the data.  As with most abstraction of this type this, the choice must be made either to support all the features of the various file formats and determine how to indicate that they original file did not support a specific feature or choose a subset of the features and throw out data from some formats when they contain data outside of this subset.  The Radar object choose the later, certain parameters available in some file formats cannot be captured by the object.  Although this places some limits on what can be done with the the class it also simplifies a use of the object in functions which operate on the class.  The various correction, plotting, and mapping routines work on radar data regardless of the source specifically because the Radar object offers a uniform representation of the data.  Dimensions do not need to be specified in the Radar and Grid classes because they are inferred from the location of the data in the class ... in most cases, some of the optional attributes are a bit more flexible for example the instrument_parameters attribute which is a bit messy.

So in short, the disconnect between the Radar object and the file is intentional.  If you do want/need fine control over a radar file you need to drop down a level of abstraction to the file specific classes (SigmetFile, MDVFile, NetCDF4.Dataset, etc).  This provide a more complete access to all the features of the file but at a loss of the user friendliness and generality of the Radar class.  

Back to the question on hand.  How should encoding to integers when writing NetCDF file (and other files?) be accomplished?  I agree option 1 is the easiest to implement but a pain to use.  Options 2 and 3 require more work but be much more powerful and simpler to use.  Since certain keys in the variable dictionaries already have special meaning, ie 'data' contains the numerical data, 'least_significant_digit' specified truncation, I would be in favor of adding a few additional keys as you mentioned.  'write_as_dtype' would work for specifying the type, others could be added for specifying compression type and level, non-default chunking, etc.  I think some of these are stored as "special" or "virtual" variable attributes in the NetCDF file and can be viewed with `ncdump -s`  The various write functions would not be required to use these if they did not support this functionality, but those that did could.  I also think that a function level parameter that would overwrite or set all of these keys may be useful.

Some reasons why I still disagree:

1) If the lower-bound is zero, the "true" phenotype is still inside the solution space. So the model is not wrong (this is the fundamental principle of constraint-based modeling).

2) Even if all organisms have some level of non-growth associated ATP costs, a model does not need to account for everything to be correct. There are many other things which are not being accounted for (e.g: regulation). All models are just incomplete by nature.

I've taken a look at this and the change itself looks good. The problem, however, is that it's backwards incompatible. Given the library is already in use and tokens have been issued, this change will result in effectively invalidating all tokens, which may be a Bad Thing depending on application level implementation details around token handling. Optimally, the library would handle this change internally and would mark the old method as deprecated. This could perhaps be achieved by using a [private claim](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-oauth-json-web-token-32#section-4.3) [replicated as a header parameter](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-oauth-json-web-token-32#section-5.3).

I *would* like to see tests added to the suite that ensure that the changes you've made are not undone (i.e. `cipher()` is called using the last half of the key and `hash()` is called with the first half). This can be achieved using mock and a custom random number generator or mocking `get_random_bytes`.

As far as API changes to allow for JSON serialization, this library was developed following the JOSE spec, but was limited to our immediate needs. As such, JSON serialization was not in the picture and would likely be a *very* low priority issue for us to tackle. If you have suggestions, feel free to log an issue. Otherwise, a patch contribution (in another PR as to not conflate this one) would likely be the most efficient way of having it addressed and released.

**Update on this:** I've figured out that the template above results in a circular dependency (which is not obvious to see). You can't use `!Ref MyBucket` in `Events` together with a policy statement containing a reference/substitution to `MyBucket` as well. I assume this is due to the Lambda Role which waits for `MyBucket` to be resolved to add the name into the policy statement and the bucket waits for the Lambda to be created so that an event notification can be added.

My solution to this problem is that I just add the bucket name directly into the resource ARN. In my case the bucket name needs to be generated like this:

```
  S3Listener:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: com.example.MyListener
      Runtime: java8
      CodeUri: target/example.jar
      Policies:
        - Version: '2012-10-17'
          Statement:
            - Effect: "Allow"
              Action: 's3:*'
              Resource:
                - !Sub
                  - arn:aws:s3:::${_bucket}       # for listObjects access
                  - { _bucket: !Join ['-', ['my', 'bucket']] }
                - !Sub
                  - arn:aws:s3:::${_bucket}/*     # for access to the bucket objects
                  - { _bucket: !Join ['-', ['my', 'bucket']] }
      Events:
        S3FileListener:
          Type: S3
          Properties:
            Bucket: !Ref MyBucket
            Events: s3:ObjectCreated:*
            Filter:
              S3Key:
                Rules:
                  - Name: suffix
                    Value: '.jpg'
```


The big problem was that my original template (containing multiple dynamo dbs, buckets and lots of SAM Lambda functions) did not say that there is such a circular dependency. It just waited endlessly to create a ChangeSet. I've just figured it out using a small test project with a simple setup: one Lambda and one bucket. In that case the error was shown directly.

This would mean "3 stacks, 1 template, 3 lambda". Let me attempt to describe this a little further.

My recommendation is to have a CloudFormation stack per environment or stage (depending on your terminology). So taking the example above, lets say we have 3 stages (dev, test, and prod). Each of those stages would have their own CloudFormation stack, lets name them as follows: dev-stack, test-stack, prod-stack. Your CI/CD system will need to deploy to dev-stack, test-stack, and then prod-stack but will be pushing one template through all of these stacks. 

Let's look go a little deeper into what is going on from a dev flow.
So Dev1, makes a change to the CloudFormation template. He or she wants to be able to add this rad traffic shifting to feature. They will edit the template, go through code review or whatever process your company follows and then push their code. Assuming everything is automated, this kicks off a deployment of the new template in the dev-stack. Dev1 validates the changes, integ tests are run, etc and then the template is deployed to the next stage. Since each stage is a CloudFormation Stack, all resources in that template will be created in each stack. This is a very common pattern and how we structure deployments internally (one stack per stage). You can take this further and have each stack in a different account. This isn't necessarily needed but I think it is a good practice to follow if it makes sense to your company or team. 

Advantages of this kind of structure:
- It limits the blast radius for any given deployment. Since everything is scoped in a CloudFormation Stack and each stack is one stage, you will never be editing production resources on accident.
- Easy replication. If you need to support multiple regions, you can replicate this easily by creating a new stack (prod-Ohio-stack and prod-Ireland-stack) in your CI/CD system and you still only manage one CloudFormation template.
- If you choose to have developers stacks, it becomes easy to replicate production by deploying the template in a new stack. (goes along with easy replication)

Caveats:
-  You may need to manage different stages differently. Maybe the dev or test stack doesn't need a prod provisioned Dynamo Table. This can be done through a combination of Stack Parameters, Conditions, and Fn::If statements. As templates grow this does become a little harder to manage (in my personally opinion).

There are probably more caveats to this but can't think of them currently.

Would be happy to discuss further. I have become very passionate about these kinds of topics (safe deployments/"Infrastructure as Code"/reduce blast radius/etc).

I would strongly oppose this approach. Adding sorting in  the indexer is not a good idea and may not be desired in some cases. Current approach allows us to process one record of the WARC file at a time and immediately spit the corresponding CDX record into a file. If we start storing the CDX output in a list for the entire WARC file then sort the list and write to a file, it will require a lot of memory for bigger collections. Additionally, if the indexes are to be stored in some key-value database, such sorting may be unnecessary.

A better approach would be to perform the CDX writing the way it is doing right now, then at the end of the script, read the newly written CDX file and sort it. However, this approach also has the limitation (same as the approach you are proposing) that only the WARC file being processed will be sorted and there will be no built-in way to merge with any existing CDX file.

If you really want to avoid the platform specific sorting tools, then you can just write a separate wrapper script that can read from STDIN, sort the lines, and spits the output to STDOUT. Then use pipes as usual.

That's what I said, running from the source is not for users, but for developers. so we can move those instructions under a separate heading to tell other developers how to run the raw code while making changes in the code and when satisfied, how to build binary from it.

Updated. I have added two functions `extend` and `extend_from_instances`:

```
def extend(self, counter: Dict[str, Dict[str, int]] = None, min_count: Dict[str, int] = None, max_vocab_size: Union[int, Dict[str, int]] = None, non_padded_namespaces: Sequence[str] = DEFAULT_NON_PADDED_NAMESPACES, pretrained_files: Optional[Dict[str, str]] = None, only_include_pretrained_words: bool = False,tokens_to_add):

def extend_from_instances(self, params: Params, instances: Iterable['adi.Instance'] = ()) :
```

The method `extend` has exact same arguments as Vocabulary initializer. The initializer creates a bare-minimum vocabulary and calls `extend` with same arguments. The method `extend_from_instances` is basically wrapper around `extend` to extend from instances. 

The `extend` method basically does the common work shared between vocabulary initialization and vocabulary extension with instances.

And fine-tune works as:
```python
vocab = model.vocab
vocab.extend_from_instances(vocabulary_params, dataset_instances))
```

I second @kipe's opinion on the filtering :) That way the ResultSet object becomes more flexible and we can easily add more filter parameters in the future. As for the multiple queries result type, what makes most sense to me is that a single query should return a single `ResultSet` object and multiple queries should return a list of `ResultSet` objects. From developer's perspective, it's really frustrating to always use expressions like `rs[0]` to get the data you need when most of the time you execute a single query. It pollutes the code and it's prone to errors.

gp2 would be slower than ephemeral due to network but you can also get Provisioned IOPS (PIOPS) if you wanted to make it a bit more consistent and closer to ephemeral (I have no real numbers at hand).

@kalefranz, more about channels. Channels are _normalized_, which means that `kalefranz` and `https://conda.anaconda.org/kalefranz` are considered the same channel (assuming default binstar channel settings).

The normalization process returns a full URL and a "canonical" channel name. The full URL, when appended with the platform or `noarch`, yields the URL where the actual packages are located. The canonical URL is the one that is used for indexing and sorting.
