Apologies for taking so long to respond.

This isn't a bug but is rather a non-intuitive behavior of the ConfigObj / Validate libraries.  ``force_list()`` is actually a Validate feature rather than a ConfigObj feature, and therefore the list forcing isn't actually applied until you call ``config.validate()``.

Here's a script which demonstrates that:

```python
from validate import Validator
from configobj import ConfigObj

spec = ConfigObj('test-87.spec', list_values=False, interpolation=False, _inspec=True)
config = ConfigObj('test-87.ini', configspec=spec)

def validate():
    validation = config.validate(Validator(), preserve_errors=True)
    if validation != True:
        print('validation errors:', validation)
        exit(1)

print('{:9} <- before validation'.format(config['xs']))
validate()
print('{:9} <- after validation'.format(config['xs']))
config.reload()
print('{:9} <- after reload, before re-validation'.format(config['xs']))
validate()
print('{:9} <- after reload, after re-validation'.format(config['xs']))
```

Here's the output of the script:
```
hello     <- before validation
['hello'] <- after validation
hello     <- after reload, before re-validation
['hello'] <- after reload, after re-validation
```

This is counterintuitive because most people wouldn't expect calling ``config.validate()`` to actually modify the ``config`` object.  Doing so is arguably a mis-feature, but for backwards compatibility we're unlikely to remove this behavior in the forseeable future.

However...

I think this whole issue is basically confusion about the difference between a token stream and an analyzer. Think of it like this: the token stream, which starts with a tokenizer and follows it with a bunch of different filters, is just a way of obtaining a stream of tokens for a given document. The analyzer, on the other hand, is responsible for turning a stream of tokens obtained from a specific document into an actual document representation. Thus, it doesn't really make sense to say something like "give me trigrams and then run stemming", because at the point you've run the analyzer, you already have your document representation. The entire token stream has already been consumed and used to create a representation. Modifying that representation after the fact isn't the job of a filter: the filters only consume a token sequence to produce a modified token sequence. They don't know how to do anything to an existing document representation. You need to make sure that your token stream contains all of the filters you want it to have before you plug it into an analyzer for producing the document representation.

When you create an analyzer, it consumes a token stream. After the point you have created the analyzer, you should just use the analyzer. It doesn't really make sense to continue using the token stream at that point, since you've already used it to create an analyzer. You don't need to manually set the content of the token stream; the analyzer will do this for you. So if you want to create an analyzer on the token stream that is "run a tokenizer, then do stemming", then the first snippet you gave is the "correct" way to do it, but you should remove the call to `set_content` as the call to `analyze(doc)` will do that for you.

Hi @datchpenguin, thanks for this.

Django does depend on an `id` field existing. This can be in a couple of ways:

1. Set one of your fields to `primary_key=True` - this will map `.id` to that field
2. Generate an `id` field in your view SQL that Django can use to uniquely identify
3. Set a generic value like `1 AS id` in your SQL if you're not interested in the ID field

Usually, it's considered good to determine a primary key for your view so you can look it up, in which case, 1 or 2 would be needed. If you never use a primary key, then just do 3 and that should work fine for most cases.

I ran into exactly this issue last night and ended up replacing the whole query with `jq` (thankfully they're the same syntax):

```bash
vm_ip_json=$(az vm list-ip-addresses \
    --name "$name" \
    --resource-group capturetest)

vm_ip=$(jq --raw-output \
    '.[0].virtualMachine.network.publicIpAddresses[0].ipAddress' \
    <<<"$vm_ip_json")
```

Workaround for anybody having the same issue.
1. Go to Azure Bash Powershell and execute your command `az aks get-credentials -g Kubernetes-RG -n Kubernetes-Cluster`
2. Copy resulting config file to cloud drive `cp .kube/config /usr/<your username>/clouddrive/config`
3. Go to your cloudshell storage account and go to Files and find your file share, download it and save it as `.kube\config`

https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#fixed-fields-13 

**discriminator**	
- **type**: string	
- **explantion**: Adds support for polymorphism. The discriminator is the schema property name that is used to differentiate between other schema that inherit this schema. The property name used MUST be defined at this schema and it MUST be in the required property list. **When used, the value MUST be the name of this schema or any schema that inherits it.**

Hi @prburgu 
Sorry for the late answer.... I was finally able to create a [unit test for VN Gateway](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/commit/2aa0301afa3d95006f94bee80112598d369e32da) to test your issue.

`public_ip_address` is taking only an id, it's why a `SubResource` is used. Assuming `self.create_public_ip()` is returning a `PublicIPAddress` instance (you didn't share this code), your preceding code should look like this:

```python
    public_ip_address = self.create_public_ip()
    subnet = self.get_subnet("GatewaySubnet")
    ip_configurations=[
           VirtualNetworkGatewayIPConfiguration(
               public_ip_address=SubResource(public_ip_address.id),
               subnet=SubResource(subnet.id),
               name=self.config.virtual_network_name
           )
    ]
```

> A warning about unclosed connections means that you did not use await close() or async with.
> From the issue, I cannot figure out why you cannot do it.

Thanks for the reply. Let me try to clarify.

Here is an example of a context manager taken directly from PEP 343 (comments are mine):

```
class locked:
   def __init__(self, lock):
       # Construction does *not* open handles/acquire resources/etc.
       # It's only used for simple initialization.
       self.lock = lock
   def __enter__(self):
       # Enter is used to open handles/acquire resources/etc.
       self.lock.acquire()
   def __exit__(self, type, value, tb):
       # Exit is used to close handles/release resources/etc.
       self.lock.release()
```

Following this, I should be able to construct (init, not enter/exit) any context manager and and discard it *without* ever touching the underlying resources. That is, the following should be completely valid:

```
my_lock = locked(lock) # Only simple initialization. No resources are acquired.
other_initialization_that_raises()
# Oops, other_initialization_that_raises() raised an Exception.
# We never get to the with statement.
# No worries, since my_lock was never __entered__, no warnings or errors are raised.
with(locked):
    do_something()
```

This, however, is not possible with aiohttp's `ClientSession`:

```
my_session = aiohttp.ClientSession() # Erroneously considered `opened` already.
assert my_session.opened, 'Session is already `opened` even though __enter__ hasn't been called.'
other_initialization_that_raises()
# Oops, other_initialization_that_raises() raised an Exception.
# We never get to the with statement.
# We get 'unclosed connection' warnings even though we haven't called __enter__ yet!
with(my_session):
    do_something()
```

```python
def get_username():
    """Get the username from environment variable NBG_USER or of the current process if NBG_USER is not set."""
    if pwd is None:
```

The checksums are ok -- that's expected behavior. If the student changes something in the test cell, then nbgrader detects the change using the checksums and replaces the cell contents with what it has stored in the database. That's strange though that the source version has a checksum and not the student version. Are you sure it isn't the other way around? I don't know how the checksum could be added to the source version since nbgrader doesn't modify that -- the checksum should only get produced in the release version after you run `nbgrader assign`.

Regardless, though, if it is working for other students then it is probably an issue with how the first one you tried got created. I'm not sure how it could have gotten out of sync in the first place (though I'd like to know, since it's probably a bug...) but to fix it I think you probably want to remove the bad submission from the database:

```
from nbgrader.api import Gradebook
gb = Gradebook("sqlite:///gradebook.db")
gb.remove_submission("week01", "Charlottehu")
```

and then try re-running `nbgrader autograde`. Hopefully that works!

Students can run the notebook that you give them with the Validate extension which will tell them if all their tests passed, but they won't see the actual grade. After you've done the autograding, you can generate feedback (with the `nbgrader feedback` command---though unfortunately this isn't currently available in the formgrader interface) and return it to students which will show them their grades.

There's currently no support for showing students grades through the assignment list interface though it's something we could conceivably add in the future.

While the gmail/github ones provide counterexamples, I still think it is much more common to use uppercase for shortcuts. I understand the idea of using lowercase, but I think we want to stick with the more common convention of uppercase. In JupyterLab for example, we have key maps setup in a way that users will eventually be able to customize shortcuts for international keyboards and the symbols shown on the shortcuts will be the ones on their keyboard. Lab also has an electron app, so the keyboard shortcuts end up also showing up in native menus where the lowercase would be very out of place. I am hesitant to change the classic notebook in ways that move it away where we are headed with lab.

Alternatively, you should be able to find the keys to manually remove in the `jupyter_notebook_config.json` (or `.py`) file(s) in one or more of the config paths in the output of `jupyter --paths`.

In Nixpkgs, when we build a package, we hardcode all paths to dependencies. For binaries, the `rpath` is updated. With interpreted code, we either wrap executables to make available executables they may need, or we patch the code to hardcode paths. That's the preferred solution and what I would like to do here. Basically, that means whenever one runs our version of `scikit-build`, it will also point to the correct `cmake` version.

in ldap_auth_provider.py  , line 168 _(fetch metadata for account creation)_:

`searchbase=self.ldap_base`

requires that user will have rights to query all attributes under ldap_base.
In our use case users do not have full permissions and can only query themselves, so no results are returned. 
We did a dirty fix and changed the query to this:
```
search_base="cn={ldap_user},{ldap_base}".format(
                         ldap_user=localpart,
                         ldap_base=self.ldap_base
                     ),
```
And that seems to fix the issue of new user registration for now. If there is a better way to get same results, please let me know.

> The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all copies or substantial portions of the Software.

So without this, flake8-bugbear appears to be violating its own license.

There already is an event loop factory fixture. It's just called `event_loop`. :) I believe mutating global state is a tricky thing; the event_loop fixture just makes an event loop for you and hands it over; closes it afterwards.

I considered your idea while I was preparing the 0.5 release, but I find the extra fixtures inelegant. (Especially fixtures that yield configuration.) Anyway, I'm not interested in discussing the subtleties of API design. What we can discuss is how to make the current design work for some of these cases.

For example, the issue of repeating yourself a lot to forbid the global event loop could be solved by http://doc.pytest.org/en/latest/example/markers.html#marking-whole-classes-or-modules. I actually thought that would work out of the box, but alas, no: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/1943

Your problem is that you can't apply the marker to a non-coroutine. So maybe we could make it so the plugin only runs coroutines in the event loop, or make a new marker that would do so.

I'm looking into simply_verify because it touches my PR #2461.

First of all, I think it is good that the responsibility to call response.simple_verify() is moved away from the plugins with this patch.

I have one comment on the code:
Would it be possible to remove the use of isinstance() to check the challenge response type? This breaks polymorphism while the only difference is the port argument. I would also note that TLSSNI01Response does not use the port argument at all.

However, I also have a more fundamental question about self-verification:
The nature the acme protocol is that it is up to the server to verify if a challenge succeeds, and it is sometimes difficult (or impossible) for the client to know that verdict in advance. https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/issues/1409#issuecomment-157308493 is related to a case of self-verification being too strict, but the other way around is also possible. Self-verification can also easily fail or pass incorrectly because of local network configuration. And in the case of dns-01, the server verification checks DNSSEC, but the user might not have a validating resolver available for the self-verification to use. How should these issues be dealt with?

Actualy... we should test this but I believe it's safe to add `[END]`.  Our HTTP -> HTTPS rewrite is going to send a 302 and cause a whole separate TCP/HTTP connection (what Apache calls an "external redirect").  If the server wants to do some multi-pass mod_rewrite stuff, it can almost certainly do that after the user comes back over HTTPS, and our rewrite with its associated `[END]` will not fire.

The only theoretical exception is if the config has a [RewriteCond](https://httpd.apache.org/docs/current/mod/mod_rewrite.html#rewritecond) containing the REQUEST_SCHEME parameter, which performs multi-pass redirects but only if REQEUST_SCHME = "http".  I think we can ignore that theoretical corner case.

Ok, I downloaded the `beagle.zip` file and was able to reproduce at least one problem. The response was a `413 - Request Entity Too Large`, which was not getting displayed by the Python SDK because the response was in html instead of JSON: ```' 413 Request Entity Too Large

413 Request Entity Too Large

nginx
'``` This should get fixed in the next release of the Visual Recognition service (timeline pending, but "soon"ish), but in the meantime the issue seems to be specific to large zip files (possibly just zip files with image files that exceed the supported size), so the workaround for this particular issue would be to use smaller image files in the zip. When you try to use one of the large images from `beagle.zip` on its own, for instance `Beagle-Rude.JPG`, you get a more sensible error response: ``` "error": { "description": "Image size limit exceeded (4129839 bytes > 2097152 bytes [2 MiB]).", "error_id": "input_error" },``` When I resized all of the images in `beagle.zip` down to a longside of 600px, the request worked. So, the service should more gracefully handle large inputs, and the SDK should relay the service's error messages, but in the meantime the solution is to use smaller images.

Interesting idea, @xLegoz!

I agree that it would be more intuitive for higher priority methods to execute first.  This just makes sense in English.  "High priority" is more urgent than "low priority".

However, I lean toward @sloria's view that the same behavior could be accomplished by writing one @post_load handler that calls several other functions.

You could even define your own @post_load_priority(priority) decorator that would register any number of methods on the Schema as (for example) self._post_load_methods.  Your @post_load function could then call all those methods in sorted order, without needing to know all the methods when the @post_load method is defined.  Subclasses could define their own methods and specify their own post_load_priority.

Another thought is, is there any way in your design to provide named hooks rather than numeric priorities?  If you have a situation where subclasses are inserting processing steps, I wonder if it'd be easier to read and maintain if you define specific steps that subclasses could hook into (which may be what @sloria meant).

> Also noticed that only can only be set at a schema level maybe it would be practical to have it like partial?

`Schema.__init__` parameters are the preferred way to configure a schema at runtime. `many` and `partial` are included in the signature for `load`/`dump` for convenience, but I'd rather not add all `__init__` parameters to load/dump.

> How could I reference a field in partial, only using something like nested.field and have it ignored, field only and not the entire group or this isn't currently possible?

Are you referring to something like the proposed API in https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/pull/405?

would it be a high level of effort to make it an addition to the typical MarshalResult() to be handled `.unknown` or something of that likeness?  I would take `PASS` and `RAISE` and opt to use @pre_* and @post_* functions to handle them (possibly for further validation to try and rectify typos, etc with then intention of extending into .data).

@adriesse to clarify:

* `bishop88()` _is_ brute force, explicit, and at least, if not more accurate than the existing methods
* `est_voc()` is a rough guess that bounds the forward bias IV curve
* Given the two methods you can interpolate or use a bisection method, _eg_: `scipy.optimize.fminbound(func=bishop88, x1=0, x2=est_voc(*args), args)` , to get any point you want with any desired tolerance and these methods will **never** blow up, ever!
* And these methods can be extended to double diode model (and already are I'm PVMismatch)

I separately implemented the Newton step because I found the canned `fminbound` method slow, but still faster than the existing singlediode method.

I personally believe the LambertW approach is unstable and less accurate compared to Bishop's method.

The main importance of plugins are to enable parallel analysis right? I heard about some guys that parallelized frame-by-frame analysis splitting the trajectories and submitting a job for each part then combining the results. They used Spark to do it on multiple computers but I don't go that far:

```sh
gmx rmsf -b    0 -e 1000 -o rmsf_0 &
gmx rmsf -b 1001 -e 2000 -o rmsf_1 &
```

With this very simple trick is possible to enable parallel analysis. The operating system takes care to allocate the resources intelligently. In most cases combining the results is just a simple concatenation as in .xvg files above. If I remember correctly from the lists the Gromacs team is also pursuing trivial analysis parallelization like this by default.

If we do:
```python
def figure_out_length(f):
    return 1000

def parallel_analysis(tool, njobs, **kwargs):
    begin = kwargs.get('b', 0)
    end = kwargs.get('e', None)
    if end is None:
        end = figure_out_length(kwargs['f'])
    
    kwargs_list = []
    count = 0
    for part_begin in range(begin, end, (end-begin)//njobs):
        part_end = part_begin + ((end-begin)//njobs) - 1
        if part_end > end:
            part_end = end
        part_kwargs = kwargs.copy()
        part_kwargs['b'] = part_begin
        part_kwargs['e'] = part_end
        for key, value in kwargs.items():
            if isinstance(value, str) and '%d' in value:
                part_kwargs[key] = value % count
        kwargs_list.append(part_kwargs)
        count = count + 1
    return kwargs_list
```

And then:
```python
from gromacs import *
parallel_analysis(rmsf, 8, f='traj.xtc', o='rmsf%d.xvg', input=['"Ca"', '"Ca"'])
```

We get a list of argument dicts where each one is for a partition of the full trajectory. If we run parallelized analysis in parallel to each other we guarantee that the machine will be with all processors in use until the end of the last analysis. Massive trivial parallization. =) =)

Speaking of over-engineering, I just published my solution to this issue.

A longer description of how I saw the problem and some issues I faced can be found at https://gist.github.com/Waino/487b96d565d2a7b31fc5#file-notes-md

The code itself is at https://github.com/Waino/Lasagne/tree/feat_columnrecurrence

I have reimplemented the original recurrent API with my system.
It passes all the tests, and seems to work on some toy problems, but runs slower than the old implementation.

The result is very complex. I don't recommend replacing the old implementation with mine, as my implementation is worthwile **only if** one needs full column recurrence. I still hope that my observations might be of some help.


I wanted to make the cells stackable within the scan, and enable full column recurrence (that goes all the way from the end of the stack at the previous time step to the start of the stack at the current time step).
I didn't want to compromise on precomputing inputs despite that.

A solution to the issue of multiple outputs is definitely needed (#337) . I vote for a dict format.

Observe that multiple output values also necessitates changes to the system for variable shape. Currently it assumes that a layer only has one output shape. I worked around it by using my custom Cell and Connection classes.

Lasagne needs better tools for accepting data (inits) either from other layers or by direct expressions. Now one needs to do a lot of instanceof checking, and worry about shape in different ways. A solution to the problem is made more complicated by the need for lazy init if shape is variable, and by special behaviors, such as repeating learnable hidden state init from row vector to n_batch.

An API is needed for storing updates returned from scan, to be passed on to Theano.function (#429) . I needed it for SamplingCell, and did a really ugly hack instead.

Stacking cells within the scan combined with full column recurrence violates one of the basic principles of the Lasagne API: because the Layers are assumed to form a DAG, the producer of a value can be handed to the initializer of the consumer.

I decided to implement pre/post-computation on a per cell basis, i.e. the whole cell is either precomputed or computed by scan. LSTMCell auto-inserts a DenseCell in front of itself, to make use of the precomputation. This means that cells don't have to implement precomputation themselves, but they must be able to function with batch and time dimensions flattened.

Ok, looking back at the issues that caused the first `StringIO` change, there were two scenarios:
1. non-ASCII text in the output caused the `style_output` preprocessor to fail on Python 2. This is [covered in the tests](https://github.com/dbcli/cli_helpers/blob/master/tests/tabular_output/test_preprocessors.py#L126:L146). This was due to our use of `cStringIO`. Fixed by using `io.StringIO`.
2. non-ASCII text in the output caused the `delimited_output_adapter` to fail on Python 2. This is [covered in the tests](https://github.com/dbcli/cli_helpers/blob/master/tests/tabular_output/test_delimited_output_adapter.py#L38:L46). This was due to our use of the standard-library `csv` module. Fixed by using `backports.csv`.

The latest issue (newline in an output row), is also [covered in the tests](https://github.com/dbcli/cli_helpers/blob/1aaa5cd6688001a7abf9ec3e79692e1cb378af13/tests/tabular_output/test_preprocessors.py#L149:L170). This was due to our use of `io.StringIO`. Fixed by using `StringIO.StringIO`.

```python
from pynwb.spec import NWBNamespaceBuilder, NWBGroupSpec, NWBAttributeSpec
from pynwb import register_class, load_namespaces, NWBHDF5IO, NWBFile

name = 'test_multicontainerinterface'
ns_path = name + ".namespace.yaml"
ext_source = name + ".extensions.yaml"

potato = NWBGroupSpec(neurodata_type_def='Potato',
                      neurodata_type_inc='NWBDataInterface',
                      doc='time of multicontainer', quantity='*',
                      attributes=[
                          NWBAttributeSpec(name='weight',
                                           doc='weight of potato',
                                           dtype='float',
                                           required=True),
                          NWBAttributeSpec(name='age',
                                           doc='age of potato',
                                           dtype='float',
                                           required=False)
                      ])

potato_sack = NWBGroupSpec(neurodata_type_def='PotatoSack',
                           neurodata_type_inc='NWBDataInterface',
                           name='potato_sack',
                           doc='test of multicontainer', quantity='?',
                           groups=[potato],
                           attributes=[
                               NWBAttributeSpec(name='help',
                                                doc='help',
                                                dtype='text',
                                                value="It's a sack of potatoes")
                           ])

ns_builder = NWBNamespaceBuilder(name + ' extensions', name)
ns_builder.add_spec(ext_source, potato_sack)
ns_builder.export(ns_path)


###

load_namespaces(ns_path)


@register_class('Potato', name)
class Potato(NWBContainer):
    __nwbfields__ = ('name', 'weight', 'age', 'source')

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(Potato, self).__init__(kwargs['source'], name=kwargs['name'])
        self.weight = kwargs['weight']
        self.age = kwargs['age']


@register_class('PotatoSack', name)
class PotatoSack(MultiContainerInterface):

    __nwbfields__ = ('potatos',)

    __clsconf__ = {
        'attr': 'potatos',
        'type': Potato,
        'add': 'add_potato',
        'get': 'get_potato',
        'create': 'create_potato',
    }

    __help = 'info about potatoes'

pmod = nwbfile.create_processing_module('module_name', 'source', 'desc')
pmod.add_container(potato_sack)


with NWBHDF5IO('test_multicontainerinterface.nwb', 'w') as io:
```

```
git mv Ganga GangaCore
find . -name '*.py' -exec sed -i 's/Ganga\./GangaCore\./g' {} \;
find . -name '*.py' -exec sed -i ':a;N;$!ba;s/import Ganga\n/import GangaCore\n/g' {} \;
```

As we explained in #393, there is no notion of password in Kinto (we'll rework the documentation).

Basic Auth has a bunch of limitations. We are aware of them but the documentation does not make them explicit (yet! Thank you for this feedback, we opened #395 to start addressing this issue.).

As a workaround, it's possible to change the permissions of the records for a specific user, by authenticating with the old `user:pass1` and adding the userid of the new `user:pass2` pair.

It is also possible to use groups to handle permissions for multiple users on multiple objects at the same time.

But ideally, for long term storage of personal data, we'd recommend using another authentication method... (see [tutorial custom auth](http://kinto.readthedocs.org/en/latest/tutorials/authentication-github.html))

Moving the discussion between @Natim and I here, instead of having it over in the [Corpix/shortid](https://github.com/corpix/shortid/issues/1#issuecomment-190329759).

I am not really sure that the merits of *ShortID* needed to get pulled into this - the example provided here using urandom() is great for an example. I think people would like to use their own ID generation strategies for all sorts of reasons. The main use case would be they want to use a common ID format and strategy between different systems/services. But, since I was originally asking how to integrate ShortID specifically into Kinto in the original IRC chat, I thought I would elaborate on the requirements I personally use regarding ID generation on my own projects, and why I initiated this request in the first place. Here is what I look for:

1. Polyglot (works in multiple languages, since I may need to use it across different micro-services)
2. Isomorphic (works across client and server so I can generate my record ID's anywhere)
3. Crypto-based strategy that reasonably ensures the required degree of nearly-true randomness
4. URL friendly (works well across HTTP boundaries)
4. Popular/Proven (leveraging the wisdom of crowds/proven in production to ensure it is sound)

The following are a number of strategies that I have found that fit this bill:

1. UUID - globally unique, proven, but large and less user friendly if you don't need *globally* unique

For strategies that don't require global uniqueness, for collection ID's I have used the following that fit the above requirements:

2. ObjectID - strategy taken from MongoDB, and now ported widely. Has the benefit of being time ordered.
3. [FirebaseID]((https://www.firebase.com/blog/2015-02-11-firebase-unique-identifiers.html) - url friendly, scalable, works, and has the benefit of supporting both time and lexigraphic ordered)
4. [ShortID](https://github.com/dylang/shortid) - growing in popularity, proven, being ported.

```python
    def searchScopeTerminal(self, blocklabel, name, current):

        varname = currentmodule[:-1] + matchedchain + ScopeLabel(name, 'signal')
```

Hello @MichaelHanssonPH ,
The Depth image PNG format is as follows - [Depth Width (pixels), Depth Height (pixels) , Compression (2 bytes), Stride (Width*2)].
Note that in the 'realsense-viewer' the depth data is post-processed to generate a colorized image with palettes like the Matlab's "Jet" and "Classic" to make it "human-readable".
The PNG snapshot, however,  preserves the original raw data (Depth, 16bit per-pixel,unsigned) that can be directly imported into 3rd party tools (e.g Matlab), and therefore is best suited for machine processing.

In case you need the colored snapshot of the depth data you can use  'rs-save-to-disk' demo that does precisely this.

Hi @Haveon, the kernel patch is configured to use the latest version for the Ubuntu LTS sources, 4.4.0-114 or later in your case, that's why the instructions are to `sudo apt dist-upgrade` before applying the patch. 
Compiling the driver with 4.4.0-114 or later but applying it on 4.4.0-98 active kernel would probably fail due to symbols mismatch  - as highlighted in the error message you receive.

In case you have you use not the latest LTS kernel then the options are:
1. Install the dkms package we provide that will patch 4.4.0-98 only - this should be the fastest way.
   Follow the [Linux Installation Guide](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/development/doc/distribution_linux.md#installing-the-packages) and apply `sudo apt-get install realsense-uvcvideo` to get dkms.
2. Manually adjust the kernel patch to fetch a specific tag within Ubuntu github tree (4.4.0-98) instead of the recommended/latest.

Yes. Our expectation was that every omitted setting in a preset / JSON would result in a default value being set. It's a bit counter-intuitive that settings made in previous presets / JSONs can have an effect on the current state.

I guess the workaround to get the desired behaviour would be to always load up a default JSON / preset first and then to load up any specific settings **on top**. Is there an API call to load up the default preset directly without going through JSON?

The best solution would be if we could specify in the JSON whether it should set the omitted values to default or whether it should keep them unchanged and use whatever was set before.

> However, I still observed quite a few new ssh connections being established even with these settings.

Do you have any insights into why? Is it because the sample `ControlPersist` times I've given above (five minutes and thirty seconds, respectively) are too short? Or something else?

> or by using a Python ssh library that does this.

Python SSH libraries are out. We looked in to Paramiko, Fabric and Ansible, and none are viable. We've discussed this extensively in #32.

> I don't think users should have to configure ssh for this to work,

SSH configuration is unavoidable. At a bare minimum, users must configure SSH keys to test against remote systems, and it is highly likely that they will also want to set a `User` and `IdentityFile`.

> especially since they may often test temporary hosts that don't have names, or that have ephemeral names.

I don't see how this is relevant. The SSH config file supports host name globbing, IP address globbing, multiple hostnames and IPs, and so on. As an example, I had entries like this in my `~/.ssh/config` for a while:

    # Pulp VMs for use by Pulp Smash.

    Host 192.168.121.*
        User vagrant
        StrictHostKeyChecking no
        UserKnownHostsFile /dev/null

    Host 192.168.121.183
        IdentityFile ~/code/pulp-dev-45/pulp/.vagrant/machines/pulp-dev-45/libvirt/private_key

> It would be ideal if pulp-smash automatically set up connection pooling

Is it, though? Yes, it'd be nice if connections were speedy and this wasn't something users had to configure. But there's up-sides to relying on the user's SSH configuration. By way of example, let's imagine that we automatically set up connection pooling using CLI flags, and the target Pulp system is configured to disallow SSH connection pooling. First, the user will be confused, because Pulp Smash SSH connections will be broken, but normal SSH connections will work fine. Second, they won't be able to override our CLI flags to fix the bug.

Relying on the user to edit `~/.ssh/config` does put more work on them. But our current approach is extremely straightforward. Users never have to ask themselves "is SSH broken because of my configuration or because of some Pulp Smash bug?" And I don't really want us to take on more responsibilities unless we have to. Saying "no" to feature requests is a great way to keep ourselves sane and doing valuable work.

The problem is `.data` doesn't get updated after you do `.save()`. So if you have a field like a `created` or `modified` auto updated timestamp, then the field isn't in there. Yes I would say that is a problem with DRF but DRF has been that way for a long time.

The other way I thought about it is you could make a new serializer.
```python
obj = serializer.save()

new_serializer = SerializerClass(instance=obj)
return cls(errors=None, **new_serializer.data)
```

However, that is incorrect too. So even if `.data` was updated it would be wrong. The problem is again if you have a DateTime, `.data` will be contain a Date String and not a DateTime Object. So the method I coded up preserves the Python objects and lets Graphene do the correct conversion.

@chshu The current KW41Z driver does not passes the ACK frame to the upper layer because the hardware is handling these frames (checks the received frame type, seqNo, CRC) and does not store them in the  packet buffer.
It is possible to pass these frames to the upper layer, but some HW acceleration needs to be disabled, and additional SW checks needs to be added.

@chshu There is no hardware feature in nRF52840 to perform encryption automatically during transmission. I thought that you wanted to use this software AES implementation in #2618 . Part of the reason we use random router ID assignment is to increase the likelihood that routers can keep the router ID when partitions merge.  If every leader uses the same deterministic method for allocating router IDs, then partitions that merge will always have router ID conflicts that need to be resolved.  If the spec says anything about router ID assignment, please let me know.

@mtn 
Hey, thanks for bringing up the issue you are having.  I wasn't aware of the ``chistributed`` framework.

My first thought is "you break it, you buy it" - as in if you choose to override a built-in command and make it do something totally and completely different in your application which inherits from ``cmd2.Cmd``, then it is your responsibility to also override any impacted methods within your class which derives from ``cmd2.Cmd`` (such as **perror()** in this particular case).  I don't view this as a failing within the ``cmd2`` framework.  For example if you don't want to see that warning you should simply override **perror** in your class to be something like:
```Python
    def perror(self, errmsg, exception_type=None, traceback_war=False):
        """ Print error message to sys.stderr and if debug is true, print an exception Traceback if one exists.

        :param errmsg: str - error message to print out
        :param exception_type: str - (optional) type of exception which precipitated this error message
        :param traceback_war: bool - (optional) if True, print a message to let user know they can enable debug
        :return:
        """
        if self.debug:
            import traceback
            traceback.print_exc()

        if exception_type is None:
            err = self.colorize("ERROR: {}\n".format(errmsg), 'red')
            sys.stderr.write(err)
        else:
            err = "EXCEPTION of type '{}' occurred with message: '{}'\n".format(exception_type, errmsg)
            sys.stderr.write(self.colorize(err, 'red'))
```

Secondly, I would say that the functionality provided by the **set** command is pretty useful.  I would recommend renaming the command provided by ``chistributed`` to be something different.  But if your command absolutely needs to be called "set", then I would copy the existing **do_set** method from ``cmd2`` and rename it to something else so your users have access to it.

Thirdly, this is Python so I'm not sure what you mean by a "privileged command that can't be overloaded".  I know how to do that in any of Java, C++, or C# but I'm not aware of how to do that in Python.  If there is a simple way to do it in Python, then please let me know how to do it and I would be happy to entertain that approach.

Lastly, if you have some good idea of how to make a simple modification to ``cmd2`` which would help you but also be generically useful to a number of other users, then please feel free to submit a Pull Request.  But honestly, this seems like it could probably be handled pretty easily on your end with a small code change.

Any thoughts?

Making it required for Python-side MI would bring it very much front and centre: in order to inherit multiply from *Python*, you would also have to bind with `py::multiple_inheritance()`, but now instead of being an annotation needed to let pybind know the C++ layout, it would be about enabling a feature on the Python side. Hi @laran,

Sounds like you are implementing an ACME server - awesome! If you haven't already read it, https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder/blob/release/docs/acme-divergences.md documents what is implemented in Boulder, which is somewhat different from the current ACME draft. You may also be interested in https://github.com/letsencrypt/pebble, which is our in-progress reference implementation for the latest version of the ACME draft. We will eventually start implementing the latest draft in Boulder.

Right now Certbot doesn't bother to implement async issuance because it wasn't specified at the time the relevant code was written, and it isn't currently supported by Boulder. Once Boulder implements the latest ACME draft, it will inherently support async issuance because that's how the latest version works. And Certbot will implement support as part of its move to the latest spec also.

> If the url contains dashes that are next to a period, the command fails saying there was an invalid character.

Dashes/hyphens at the "outside" of labels are prohibited according to [RFC 1035](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1035#section-2.3.1):

> The labels must follow the rules for ARPANET host names.  They must start with a letter, end with a letter or digit, and have as interior characters only letters, digits, and hyphen.

Although [later](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2181#section-11), the application of DNS was widened (including binary), this probably wouldn't be the case for the use of hostnames, like in the PKIX system.

Hey all, thanks for the feedback. @old-bear, I started to respond, but I think you raise a good point. 

Here is a proposal that I think should clarify the issues that you bring up: 

## Class hierarchy:

 * **SearchAlgorithm()**: This is the interface and will not change
   * **BasicTrialGenerator(SearchAlgorithm)**: This is the base class for Trial generation. It is used for handling the Experiment/JSON interface. It offers `_create_trial` and `_generate_trials` functionality, in addition to a separate function that isolates the variant generation.
     * **SuggestionAlgorithm(BasicTrialGenerator)**: This inherits from BasicTrialGenerator but overrides the variant generation feature with a more simplistic `_suggest` interface. This ensures that the semantics of variant generation of variant generation do not overlap with the semantics of a separate search utility.
       * **HyperOptSearch(SuggestionAlgorithm)**: Overrides `_suggest`.
       * **SigOptSearch(SuggestionAlgorithm)** Overrides `_suggest`.

## Code
This proposal will extend the current PR.
```python

class BasicTrialGenerator(SearchAlgorithm):
    def _generate_trials(self, unresolved_spec, output_path=""):
        self._parser = make_parser()  # this will actually go into init
        if "run" not in unresolved_spec:
            raise TuneError("Must specify `run` in {}".format(unresolved_spec))
        for _ in range(unresolved_spec.get("repeat", 1)):
            yield _create_trial_variant(unresolved_spec)

    def _create_trial_variant(self, unresolved_spec, output_path=""):
        for resolved_vars, spec in generate_variants(unresolved_spec):
            experiment_tag = str(self._counter)
            if resolved_vars:
                experiment_tag += "_{}".format(resolved_vars)
            yield self._create_trial(spec, output_path, experiment_tag=experiment_tag)

    def _create_trial(self, spec, output_path, **kwargs):
        try:
            # Special case the `env` param for RLlib by automatically
            # moving it into the `config` section.
            if "env" in spec:
                spec["config"] = spec.get("config", {})
                spec["config"]["env"] = spec["env"]
                del spec["env"]
            args = self._parser.parse_args(to_argv(spec))
        except SystemExit:
            raise TuneError(
                "Error parsing args, see above message", spec)
        if "trial_resources" in spec:
            kwargs["resources"] = json_to_resources(spec["trial_resources"])
        self._counter += 1
        return Trial(
            trainable_name=args.run,
            config=args.config,
            local_dir=os.path.join(args.local_dir, output_path),
            stopping_criterion=args.stop,
            checkpoint_freq=args.checkpoint_freq,
            restore_path=args.restore,
            upload_dir=args.upload_dir,
            max_failures=args.max_failures,
            **kwargs)

class SuggestionAlgorithm(BasicTrialGenerator):
    def _create_trial_variant(self, unresolved_spec, output_path=""):
        trial_id = Trial.generate_id()
        while True:
            suggested_config = self._suggest(new_config, trial_id)
            if suggested_config is None:
                yield None
            else:
                break
        spec = copy.deepcopy(unresolved_spec)
        spec["config"] = suggested_config
        yield self._create_trial(spec, output_path, trial_id=trial_id)

```

Fixed by #822. The approach was to build (but not install) pyarrow (as part of `build.sh`), then copy the `pyarrow` directory to `ray/python/ray/pyarrow_files/pyarrow`. Then in `ray/python/ray/__init__.py`, we add `ray/python/ray/pyarrow_files` to the python path so that `import pyarrow` finds the right pyarrow.

Regarding the naming scheme, I think the parameter should just be called `retry-max` instead of `retry-fetch-max`, because it is shorter and only contains one dash. Renaming the other parameters shouldn't be done, as it is unnecessary and would also require a major version bump which we can't do now.

Then we would have `retry-streams`, which is responsible for fetching the streams multiple times as a "boolean" param with `delay` seconds in between, `retry-max` to limit the `number` of fetches and `retry-open` for setting the `number` of attempts for the selected stream to be opened.

Additionally, I think that `retry-max` should set `retry-streams` to 1, if it is not set. Does that make sense?

I've slept on this and I think @retke has a point - some relatively large files which are only installation-dependent, not instance-dependent, are copied into the instance's data directory. However, I don't think the data manager is the place to deal with this, as the data manager is *specifically* for getting data and metadata for the current instance. Moreover, this use of the data manager could cause some concerning issues when the following situation occurs:
 - Two instances are running on the same system
 - Both instances have a 3rd party cog installed which utilises global data
 - The 3rd party cog in question is updated on one instance but not the other, possibly causing the cogs to conflict with each other on their treatment of the global data

It's important that different revisions of any cog can guarantee compatibility with any bundled data. This is why bundled data is *always* instance-dependent, along with any cog installations.

Now, as for the Lavalink.jar being copied, I think it is a different issue, and it still needs discussion. Personally, I think `red-lavalink` could bundle the Lavalink.jar, but I might be overseeing something in that design.

Hello,

Thank you for your reply @petewa. I played a little bit with the suggested changes and with the two branches (`develop` and `csiro/execution-engine`), producing benchmarks to qualify and quantify the results. Since there are just too many results to post them here, I give an overall impression of them. The comments below refer to loading times of 9 Lansat 8 time slices, sharing the comments for both branches (unless specified otherwise) and in an Amazon EC2 t2.xlarge instance (vcPU: 4, memory: 16 GiB). Also, please note that when I refer to "tile size" I am referring to the ratio "tile size/resolution" (I did not change the resolution), and when referred to a chunking parameter, the chunks are squared (therefore a chunking parameter of `200` stands for `200x200`).
* If only the chunking parameter is changed from the original `200` to `2000`: the loading time is much faster (40%-85% faster!), specially if `use_threads=True`.
* If only the tile size is changed from `3500` to `8000`: it is faster, but much slower than only changing the chunking parameter from `200` to `2000`.
* If both the tile size is changed from `3500` to `8000` and the chunking parameter from `200` to `2000`: it is faster, but the loading times are similar to only changing the chunking parameter, as if the tile size did not have an enhancing effect.
* Having played with the above parameters, the largests files in the bucket are slighly larger than 5 MB. I guess other parameters should be used to obtain objects around 10-20 MB in size.
* When ingesting to the EBS, there is not a clear difference with `use_threads` with either the default NetCDF driver or the S3-file driver.
  The way I ingest to the EBS with the S3-file driver is the following:  
  * For each ingestion configuration file (so different tile sizes and chunkings) I set `driver: s3` in the `storage:` list, i.e.
    ```
    storage:
        driver: s3
        crs: EPSG:4326
        tile_size:
            longitude: 0.943231048326
            latitude:  0.943231048326
        resolution:
            longitude: 0.000269494585236
            latitude: -0.000269494585236
        chunking:
            longitude: 200
            latitude: 200
            time: 1
        dimension_order: ['time', 'latitude', 'longitude']
    ``` 
  * Ingest with the usual command, `datacube -v ingest -c ~/ls8_test.yaml`. If I run `datacube --driver s3 -v ingest -c ~/ls8_test.yaml`, I get the error `datacube.utils.DatacubeException: Missing 'container' parameter, cannot write to storage.` I understand that, if I set the container parameter, the data will be ingested to the S3 bucket rather than into the EBS. Am I right?
* When ingesting to S3, the branches `develop` and `csiro/execution-engine` result approximately equal in timewise terms, except when the chunking parameter is set small, i.e. `200`, regardless of the tile size. In these cases, the differences are like the ones in the table of my previous comment.
* The fastest loading times from data ingested in the S3 bucket is given by the branch develop, with `use_threads=True`, and with chunking set to `2000`, regardless of the tile size. It is also quicker to load data chunked to `2000` with `use_threads=False` than data chunked to `200` and `use_threads=True`.

Now everything makes more sense. @petewa says in the previous comment that `use_threads` should make a big difference when ingesting to EBS with the S3-file driver, in contrast with using the default NetCDF driver. If we do find a reason to do this, I think it should be as a low-level Linux-specific API, similar to the low-level kqueue API (#578). There's just no consistency across systems about what counts as "priority" or "exceptional" data, or how it's handled – for example, kqueue doesn't even have a (portable) way to do `POLLPRI`, and then there was that time that Apple tried to [add support for urgent-data...](https://sandstorm.io/news/2015-04-08-osx-security-bug)

@richardcode @chrisroadmap 

A few thoughts on some potential rules/guidelines:

- Use test driven development. In very brief it means write your tests first, only write enough code to pass the tests, refactor the code once it's passing so you leave things tidier than when you started, repeat. If you want to read more have a look at [this nature article](https://www.nature.com/news/collaborative-software-development-made-easy-1.22729) or [this intro guide](https://code.tutsplus.com/tutorials/the-newbies-guide-to-test-driven-development--net-13835) (although even that is fairly long). If you want to read more in detail have a look [(here](https://technologyconversations.com/2013/12/20/test-driven-development-tdd-example-walkthrough/) or [here](http://agiledata.org/essays/tdd.html). 
    - The tests will also act as documentation and ensure consistency.
- Create a style guide
- Avoid code duplication wherever possible. A possible second step for us is to use a code analyser/linter to highlight code duplication where it occurs and run analysis of our code before any merges are executed (i.e. add linting to our continuous integration).
- Take particular care to have the code say **what** it does clearly. Only resort to comments where you cannot explain **why** you are doing something any other way.
- Limit methods to a maximum of 10 lines except in exceptional circumstances. Never ever more than 50 lines. Similarly try and have classes be responsible for one thing and one thing only. With this rule in mind, then try to have as few classes as possible.

Most of these thoughts from two books:
- [Clean Code by Robert Martin](https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/3735293-clean-code)
- [Refactoring by Martin Fowler](https://martinfowler.com/books/refactoring.html)

Right now I don't really care about documentation as the code should be fairly self-documenting if we follow the above. When we do get round to it we should work out how to use `sphinx` properly but that's a fiddle which is beyond me right now.

If it can help someone else, I updated the **docker-compose.yml** file by adding a service that listen to docker events and connect the lambda containers to the **local_default** network automatically (the network name could be different)

Here is the service in **docker-compose.yml**

```
services:

  localstack:
    ...

  docker-events-listener:
    build:
      context: build
    volumes:
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
    privileged: true
```

Here is the **Dockerfile** in the build folder

```
FROM docker:18.06.0-ce
RUN apk add --no-cache bash
COPY listen-docker-events.sh /var/listen-docker-events.sh
CMD ["/bin/bash", "/var/listen-docker-events.sh"]
```

Here is the content of **listen-docker-events.sh** in the build folder

```
#!/bin/bash

docker events --filter 'event=create' --filter 'type=container' --format '{{.Actor.Attributes.name}}' | while read container_name

do
    echo "$container_name was created"

    # Connect any container whose name contains localstack_lambda_ to the local_default network
    if [[ $container_name = *"localstack_lambda_"* ]]; then
        docker network connect localstack_default $container_name --link localstack
        echo "The container $container_name is now connected to the local_default network"
    fi
done

```

I compared the download speeds under Windows 10 and Python 3.6.1 and most recent `pycurl`, `curl`, etc. from `conda-forge` between `v0.10` and this PR's version.

Method: simply taking turn between two identical CLI calls, eye-balling the speeds over a couple of minutes each time.

Result: There was **no difference in the speeds**. In my case, both were around 5 MB/s.

I don't know how you could make a more accurate comparison. Maybe one could have two parallel downloads on different machines on the same internet connection over a longer time, comparing how many products of the same selection each version gets downloaded. But there are so many factors to control...

There certainly are no extreme differences.

Thanks a lot! I've been going through some of my other code and it was common I was taking for granted where my module was running by using 'os.getcwd()' or something else. For the onefile PyInstaller you can check if running in a "frozen" state, which for windows means it is running from a temp folder in ~/AppData/Local/Temp/randomGenName

You can check for being frozen:
```python
# PyInstaller considerations
if getattr(sys, 'frozen', False):
        # we are running in a bundle
        bundle_dir = sys._MEIPASS
else:
        # we are running in a normal Python environment
        bundle_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
```

wrote:
> My PR making the tiny changes that help tornado was accepted, so with the next tornado release, the ZMQPoller will work entirely without issue.
>
> The only case where the pyzmq IOLoop and tornado 2.1.2 IOLoop with our ZMQPoller will behave differently is if you terminate the *context* of one or more active sockets while the IOLoop is running, the pyzmq IOLoop will catch this error and exit cleanly, while the tornado IOLoop will let the ZMQError raise.  This is probably an extremely rare case

Great, I remember this extra logic that we had in the pyzmq IOLoop
implementation.

> I can draw up a couple of examples for working with tornado, but right now, you can use pyzmq with tornado with:
>
> ```python
> from tornado.ioloop import IOLoop
> from pyzmq.eventloop.ioloop import ZMQPoller
>
> loop = IOLoop(ZMQPoller())
> loop.install() # registers as global tornado _instance
> ```
>
> I did not do it this way in the `install()` function, because the little changes to error logging just merged into tornado are really necessary for a reasonable experience if there are errors.  After the next tornado release, I may reverse the `install() / _instance` logic to create a tornado IOLoop and set it as the pyzmq instance, rather than vice versa, so we stop doing anything unofficial with tornado at all.
